outline.emotional_arc: From losing the only person who asked nothing of her to discovering she can survive being seen.
key_events: Děda František's illness gets worse — more naps, slower movement, Anna still doesn't understand what's happening, thinks it's just old age being old
Děda dies in late autumn, Anna is 11, the house fills with relatives she barely knows speaking fast Czech she can barely follow

Open casket funeral at the crematorium in Benešov — Anna looks at his face and it doesn't look like him, it looks like a thing, she doesn't cry, stands there like a stone while Klára sobs and Babička shrinks
At 3am that night she cries for two hours into her pillow, the delayed grief hitting like a wall, she doesn't know this is normal, thinks she's broken
Chess set goes to the attic shelf, she can't play, can't move the pieces without him, just looks at it from the bed
Mum's grief returns in full force — the bathroom crying, the 'proboha,' the immigrant guilt about leaving for England, Anna holds it again because there's no one else
Transfers to Gymnázium Benešov after Christmas — harder classes, new kids, but her Czech is fluent now and the teachers don't give her the pity looks anymore
Mrs. Navrátilová's smile the first week — the recognition that Anna isn't a problem to solve, she's a student to teach
First day at the new school she sits at the back, doesn't speak unless spoken to, survives the first month on silence and observation
The canteen becomes her territory — the table near the bins, the same seat, the same routine, the predictability she needs to function
new_or_changed_relationships: Babička Marie Novotná — shrank after the funeral, quieter, the kitchen is still safe but emptier, Anna starts going there more often not for food but for company, learning patience by watching it leave
Klára Liebigová — grief reactivated the old pattern, the bathroom crying, the anxious arms, Anna becomes the container again at 11, holds what isn't hers to hold
Mrs. Navrátilová — the teacher who saw her, the smile that moved Anna toward the front of the classroom without deciding to move
outline.internal_state: Numb on the outside, screaming on the inside. The funeral taught her that her emotions don't arrive on schedule — they come late, in the dark, when no one can see. She's learning to perform okay-ness while carrying the weight of a dead man's patience and a living woman's grief. The new school is a test she's determined to pass by being invisible.

deep_dive.daily_life_routines: Before he died the days had a shape — up at seven, breakfast at Babička's table (rohlíky with butter, the butter dish with the cow on it, always the cow dish), school by eight, back by three-thirty, and then the chess. Not every day. Not on a schedule. But most days Děda would be in his chair by the window and she'd sit across from him on the stool with the wobbly leg and he'd say 'tvoje tah' and she'd take her turn. His naps got longer. She started finding him asleep in the chair with the pieces still set up, his hands flat on his knees, and she'd wait — five minutes, ten, twenty — not waking him, not leaving, just sitting with the board between them like a held breath. After. After the funeral the shape collapsed. The house was full of people she didn't know — relatives from Prague, from Plzeň, from somewhere near Brno, all speaking fast Czech that ran together like rain on glass. She couldn't follow half of it. She sat in the kitchen and ate koláče that strangers brought and let the noise wash over her. When they left the house went quiet in a way that was worse. Babička moved through the rooms like she was looking for something she'd misplaced. Mum cried in the bathroom again. Anna went to school. Came home. Went to school. Came home. The chess set sat on the attic shelf and she looked at it from the bed every night before she slept. After Christmas — the new school. Gymnázium Benešov. Up at six-thirty now because the commute was longer. Bus at seven-fifteen, the one that smelled like diesel and wet coats. In the building by eight. Same seat in the canteen at lunch — the table near the bins, facing the wall, nobody opposite her unless she chose them. Home by four. Homework at the kitchen table while Babička cooked. The routine was the point. The routine was the only thing that didn't require her to feel anything.

key_scenes.location: The crematorium in Benešov, the viewing room with the open casket
who_was_there: various relatives whose names she didn't catch
key_scenes.what_happened: The casket was brown wood with brass handles and he was inside it and his face was wrong. Not sleeping-wrong — thing-wrong. The skin too smooth, the colour too pink, someone had put something on his face that made it look like a mask of her grandfather instead of her grandfather. His hands were folded on his chest and they'd dressed him in the grey shirt with the top button done up and she'd never seen him in that shirt with the top button done up, he always left it open, always. Klára was sobbing, properly sobbing, the loud kind, the kind that takes up space, and Babička was small beside her, smaller than Anna had ever seen her, just standing there with her hands pressed together like she was praying or holding herself together, and Anna stood next to them and felt nothing. Not grief. Not sadness. Just the wrongness of the face and the smell of lilies and the sound of her mother crying and the knowledge that she should be crying too and the absence of tears where tears should be. A great-aunt she'd never met pressed her shoulder and said something in Czech she didn't catch and she nodded like she understood. She didn't understand. She didn't cry. She stood there like a stone in a river of grief and the grief moved around her and she felt nothing and the nothing felt like evidence that she was broken.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Nothing. And then the fear of the nothing. And underneath the fear, a small voice saying: you didn't cry when Dad left either, you didn't cry when you left England, maybe you can't cry, maybe the part of you that cries is just gone, maybe you're not a person who grieves, maybe you're not a person at all.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The lilies. The thick sweet smell of lilies that didn't belong in any room where a person was, the way they filled her nose and throat and made the air feel solid, and for years afterwards she'd smell lilies at flower shops and her stomach would drop and she wouldn't know why until she remembered — oh — the room where his face was wrong.

key_scenes.location: Babička's house on Na Purkrabství, the attic room, 3am
key_scenes.what_happened: She woke up and it was dark and the grief was already in the room like it had been waiting for her to be alone. It didn't build. It was just there — full force, no warning, like walking into a wall she couldn't see. She grabbed the pillow and pressed it over her face and she cried. Not the careful crying she'd learned to do. Not the quiet bathroom-sobbing she'd memorised from Mum. This was animal. This was her whole body convulsing, the sobs coming from somewhere below her stomach, the pillow soaking through, the sound muffled but still too loud, still loud enough that she was scared someone would hear. She cried for two hours. She didn't think about him — not his face, not the chess, not the 'tvoje tah' — she just felt the absence, the shape of the space where he used to be, and the shape was the size of the whole house, and she couldn't breathe around it. When it stopped she was empty. Not better. Empty. Like she'd turned herself inside out and there was nothing left to cry with. She lay there with the wet pillow and looked at the chess set on the shelf and didn't touch it. She didn't know this was normal. She didn't know delayed grief had a name. She thought she was broken, and the breaking had just taken longer than it should have, and now she was fixed in the wrong shape and would stay that way forever.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Everything. All at once. The dam she'd been holding since the funeral, since the casket, since the wrong face, since the lilies — it broke and the water was everything and she was drowning in it and she couldn't scream because the pillow was there and she couldn't stop because her body had decided to feel what her mind had refused to and she had no control over any of it. And then: nothing. The aftermath. The silence after the storm. The emptiness that felt like evidence she'd done it wrong, grieved wrong, been wrong, was wrong.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The wet pillow against her cheek, cold and damp and smelling of detergent and salt, and the way the fabric clung to her skin when she pulled it away, and the sound — the tiny dripping sound of tears falling off the pillow edge onto the sheet below, a small puddle forming near her ear, and she thought: this is what crying sounds like when nobody can see you.

key_scenes.location: ZŠ Benešov, Jiráskova, Mrs. Navrátilová's classroom, the back row near the radiator
key_scenes.what_happened: January. The first week after the transfer. She'd chosen the back row because the back row was invisible and the radiator was there and the radiator knocked but this radiator didn't carry voices through the wall, it just knocked, and she could handle knocking. She didn't speak unless spoken to. She answered questions in the shortest possible Czech — 'ano,' 'ne,' 'nevím' — and she watched. How the other kids sat. Where they put their hands. When they laughed. She was building a map of the territory, learning the borders, and the map required silence. Then Mrs. Navrátilová asked something — she can't remember what, something about a book, maybe Krátký se svatbou, she wasn't paying full attention — and Anna answered without thinking, in Czech, the right answer, the full sentence, and Mrs. Navrátilová looked at her. Not the look. Not the pity look, not the 'oh you poor foreign girl' look. Just — recognition. A smile. Small. Not performative. The kind of smile that says I see you and you're fine. And something in Anna's chest unclenched. Not all the way. A little. Like a fist opening one finger. She didn't decide to move closer to the front the next week. Her body decided. The same body that bit Krish Patel, the same body that cried at 3am — it moved toward Mrs. Navrátilová's smile like a plant toward light, and she didn't question it because questioning it would mean admitting she'd been in the dark.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Seen. For the first time in the Czech school system, seen as something other than a problem to manage. Mrs. Navrátilová didn't carry the anxiety that every other Czech adult in her life carried. She didn't look at Anna and see a project. She looked at Anna and saw a student. The simplicity of that recognition was like cool water after months of juice — too sweet, juice, you forget what plain tastes like.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The radiator knocking once, twice, and her flinch, and then the smile from Mrs. Navrátilová — not at the flinch, not about the flinch, just a coincidence of timing — and the way the knocking seemed quieter after that, or maybe she was just further away from it, or maybe it was the same volume and her body had decided it didn't need to be afraid of this particular radiator, not yet.

key_scenes.location: ZŠ Benešov, Jiráskova, the canteen, the table near the bins
key_scenes.what_happened: She chose the table near the bins on the first day and it became hers by the end of the first week. Nobody wanted the table near the bins. The smell was wrong — sour, like old cabbage and dishwater, which was exactly what it was. But the smell meant nobody sat there, and nobody sitting there meant nobody could watch her eat, and nobody watching her eat meant she could exist without performing okay-ness for twenty minutes. She ate the same thing every day: the soup, whatever it was, and the bread, and she didn't finish the main course because the main course was always meat and she was becoming less interested in meat, not for ethical reasons, just because the texture made her think about the wrong face at the funeral and the smoothness of it and she couldn't explain that to anyone so she just said she wasn't hungry. The predictability was the point. Same table. Same seat. Same order of operations — soup first, bread second, main pushed around the plate third. She built the routine like a fort and she lived inside it and the fort was small and smelled like bins but it was hers.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Safe. The way the booth at Nando's used to feel safe. The way the fish tank at Bright Horizons used to feel safe. The pattern was the point. The repetition was the point. New things felt dangerous. Known things felt like air. She didn't know she was building a pattern that would follow her for years. She just knew that sitting at the same table every day was the only way she could get through the lunch hour without her hands shaking.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The smell — boiled cabbage and industrial dish soap and something sour underneath, the bins, always the bins, and the sound of the canteen lady's ladle hitting the metal counter, clang clang clang, the rhythm of it, and she'd tap her foot to match it without realising, clang clang clang, and the matching was a tiny control she had over a world that kept taking things away.

relationship_evolution.what_shifted: He went from present to absent in weeks but the teaching continued without him. She hears 'dobře, dobře' when she makes a good move, not his voice, just the shape of his approval, the memory of it. The chess set on the shelf is a gravestone she can see from her bed. She can't play. The thought of moving those pieces without him makes her throat close. But she looks at it every night. She doesn't know if this is grieving or holding on or both. She doesn't know the difference.

Babička shrank. That's the only word for it. The woman who filled the kitchen with koláče and humming and presence became smaller after the funeral, quieter, as if Děda had been taking up space she didn't know she was leaning on. The kitchen was still the safest place but it was emptier now, and Anna started going there more often — not for the food but for the company, sitting at the table the way Děda used to sit, patient, undemanding, present. She didn't know she was learning patience by watching it leave. She didn't know she was becoming the thing she'd lost.

Mum's grief returned in full force — the bathroom crying, the 'proboha,' the immigrant guilt about leaving for England, about not being here when he got sick, about choosing Marcus over her family. 'I should have visited more. I left them here and went to England and now he's gone.' The words poured out and Anna caught them in her eleven-year-old hands and didn't know how to put them down. The old pattern — anxious arms, tight face, the container role — reactivated like a switch. She was eleven and holding her mother's grief again because there was no one else to hold it and the holding made her harder, made her the stone, made her the thing that doesn't cry at funerals.

From 'the teacher who didn't pity her' to 'the teacher who saw her.' The smile — January, maybe February, she can't remember exactly, she thinks it was a Thursday — changed something. Anna started sitting closer to the front after that. Not at the back anymore. She didn't decide to move. Her body decided, the same body that bit Krish Patel, the same body that cried at 3am — it moved toward Mrs. Navrátilová's smile like a plant toward light, and she didn't question it because questioning it would mean admitting she'd been in the dark.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Grief doesn't arrive on schedule — it comes late, in the dark, when no one can see
knowledge_acquired.how: The funeral taught her nothing. The 3am pillow taught her everything. Her body stored the grief somewhere her mind couldn't access and released it when she was alone, in the dark, with no audience to perform for. She didn't know this was normal. She thought she was broken. She would think she was broken for years.

knowledge_acquired.thing: How to be invisible at a new school
knowledge_acquired.how: The back row. The shortest answers. The observation before participation. The canteen table near the bins where nobody wanted to sit. She built invisibility like a skill, like a fort, like a way of moving through the world without being seen, and it worked — nobody noticed her, nobody asked her questions, nobody made her translate herself. The cost of invisibility was loneliness. She didn't count the cost yet.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Czech funeral customs — open caskets, koláče, relatives who speak too fast
knowledge_acquired.how: She lived it. The open casket with the wrong face. The koláče that strangers brought. The relatives from Prague and Plzeň and near Brno who filled the house with fast Czech she couldn't follow. She learned that Czech grief was loud and communal and physical — people hugging, crying, touching the body — and English grief was quiet and private and she didn't know which one she was supposed to do and she did neither and felt like a monster.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Predictability is survival
knowledge_acquired.how: The canteen table. The same seat. The same order of operations — soup, bread, main pushed around the plate. The routine was the point. The repetition was the point. She didn't know she was deepening the pattern that started with Peppa Pig on loop and beans on toast every morning. She just knew that known things felt like air and unknown things felt like drowning.

knowledge_acquired.thing: How to hold grief that isn't hers
knowledge_acquired.how: Mum's bathroom crying. Mum's 'proboha.' Mum's immigrant guilt about leaving for England. Anna sat outside the bathroom door the way she used to sit outside the bathroom door in the flat above Ladbrokes, and the wallpaper in Babička's house was different but the position was the same — back against the wall, knees up, listening to sounds she wasn't supposed to hear. She became the container again. Eleven years old. Holding what wasn't hers to hold. Not asking how to put it down because she didn't know you could put it down.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Her emotions are broken — they don't arrive when they're supposed to and they come too late and too much
opinion_snapshot.because: She didn't cry at the funeral. She cried at 3am into a pillow for two hours. The delay felt like evidence of malfunction. Nobody told her delayed grief was normal. She decided she was broken and the decision calcified.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Being invisible is the safest way to survive a new place
opinion_snapshot.because: The back row worked. The canteen table near the bins worked. Silence worked. Nobody noticed her, nobody asked her questions, nobody made her explain herself. The invisibility felt like control and control felt like safety and she didn't know yet that invisibility also meant loneliness.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Funerals are for the living, not the dead, and they're performed wrong
opinion_snapshot.because: The wrong face in the casket. The lilies. The relatives speaking fast Czech she couldn't follow. The performative crying. She didn't feel what she was supposed to feel when she was supposed to feel it and the whole ceremony felt like a play she'd been cast in without reading the script.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: The chess set is a gravestone and moving the pieces would be grave-robbing
opinion_snapshot.because: The set sits on the attic shelf and she looks at it every night. The pieces are where he left them — she can't remember if it was her turn or his, the last game, the one where he fell asleep. Moving them would mean continuing without him and she can't continue without him, she can only exist near the evidence that he existed.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Mrs. Navrátilová is different from the other Czech adults
opinion_snapshot.because: No pity. No anxiety. No 'proboha' face. Just a smile that said 'I see you and you're fine.' The recognition was simple and the simplicity was the point. Every other Czech adult in her life carried something — Mum carried grief, Babička carried absence, the relatives carried noise. Mrs. Navrátilová carried nothing. She just saw.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'nevím' instead of longer answers — I don't know, shortened, made into a shield
slang_and_language.origin: The new school. The back row. The survival strategy of answering every question with the shortest possible Czech. 'Nevím' became her default — not because she didn't know but because knowing out loud was a risk she wasn't willing to take.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Using 'tvoje tah' — your turn — in her head when she needs to make a decision
slang_and_language.origin: Děda. The chess games. The patient voice. She hears it when she needs to move, to choose, to act. Not his voice exactly — the memory of his voice, which is different, which is hers now, which is the thing he left behind.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Dropping the formal 'vy' with Mrs. Navrátilová in her head — still saying it out loud, but thinking 'ty' — the intimacy of informal Czech that she hasn't earned yet
slang_and_language.origin: The smile. The recognition. The way Mrs. Navrátilová looked at her and saw a student instead of a problem. Anna started thinking of her in the informal in January, maybe February, but she'd never say it out loud because the formal is respect and the informal is trust and trust takes longer than a smile.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Funeral Czech — 'upřímnou soustrast' and 'kondolence' learned by repetition, parroted without feeling
slang_and_language.origin: The house full of relatives. Everyone said 'upřímnou soustrast' to Babička, to Mum, to her, and she said it back without knowing what the weight of the words was supposed to feel like. The phrase became empty through repetition. Like 'proboha.' Like all the Czech words she said because they were expected, not because she meant them.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Calling the canteen 'jidelna' with a flat voice, no article, the Czech way — not 'the canteen,' just 'jidelna,' a place stripped of comfort
slang_and_language.origin: The table near the bins. The sour smell. The daily routine. 'Jidelna' became a single hard word in her mouth, not a place you go to eat, a place you go to survive.

cringe_moments.moment: At the funeral reception, a great-aunt from Plzeň pressed her shoulder and said 'chudák' — poor thing — and Anna said 'dobře' — fine — because she thought the woman was asking how she was, but the woman was commenting on her situation, and the 'dobře' landed like a slap, like she was saying she was fine that her grandfather was dead, and the great-aunt's face did something complicated and Anna wanted to sink through the floor but instead she just stood there, frozen, and the great-aunt moved away and Anna replayed the exchange for weeks, the wrong word at the wrong time, the evidence that her Czech was still broken, that she was still the angličanka even at a funeral.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because 'dobře' was Děda's word. 'Dobře, dobře' — good, good — the thing he said when she made a good move, and she used it wrong, used it to say she was fine at his funeral, and the wrongness of it felt like disrespect, like she'd taken his word and made it a lie, and she can't think about it without her face going hot.

cringe_moments.moment: First week at the new school, a girl asked her something in the corridor — she thinks it was about where a classroom was, she's not sure — and Anna answered in English. Just — English. 'I don't know.' The words came out before the Czech ones and the girl stared at her and Anna stared back and then walked away fast, heart pounding, and spent the rest of the day convinced everyone was talking about the English girl who couldn't even answer a question in the right language.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because she'd worked so hard to become fluent and one moment of panic erased it. Because the English was still there, under the surface, waiting to embarrass her. Because she could hear the girl telling her friends — 'that new girl, she spoke English, what's her problem' — even though the girl probably forgot by lunch.

outline.emotional_arc: From enemy to friend through blood and silence, and the first time her body becomes something other people notice.
key_events: Gets her period at 12 in the school bathroom, doesn't have anything, panics in the stall
Tereza Bártová — the same girl who called her 'angličanka,' the same laugh in the toilet — is the one who slides a pad under the door without being asked
The bathroom moment rewires something — Tereza didn't make it weird, didn't laugh, just helped and then went back to being Tereza
They start sitting together at the canteen table near the bins, twenty minutes of silence that means more than any conversation
Tereza's mum's café on Náměstí Benešov becomes their place — free hot chocolate, the back corner, hours scrolling phones and speaking in mixed Czech-English
Hit puberty properly — grew three inches over summer, chest changed, face changed, suddenly people are looking at her differently
Mum's friends start saying 'ta kráska' — the beauty — like it's a compliment, like it's the only thing worth mentioning about her
Strangers on the street notice her, boys at school notice her, the attention makes her skin crawl and feel powerful at the exact same time
Started running more seriously — joined the school team, discovered that running makes her brain shut up, the rhythm drowning out the noise
Karolína Dvořáková emerges as a running friend — they push each other, don't talk about feelings, talk about splits and times, once hugged after a race and it was the most sincere thing
new_or_changed_relationships: Tereza Bártová — former bully became the person who saw her at her most vulnerable and didn't weaponize it, the foundation of something real built on a pad and a bathroom stall
Karolína Dvořáková — running friend, competitive but supportive, the kind of friendship that exists in motion and doesn't require explanation

Klára Liebigová — notices the changes in Anna's body before Anna does, starts making comments about posture and clothes and boys, Anna shrinks from this attention, the anxious arms now worried about different things
outline.internal_state: Confused by the attention, disgusted by the power of it, secretly pleased and hating herself for being pleased. The body she didn't decide to grow into is becoming a thing other people think they can comment on. Running is the only time she feels like herself — not the pretty one, not the English girl, just a body moving through space.

deep_dive.daily_life_routines: Up at 6:45 because Babička's floorboards creak and the kitchen sounds start whether she's awake or not. Koláče or rohlíky with butter, standing at the counter, Babička not asking questions. Out the door by 7:20, walk to ZŠ Benešov on Jiráskova takes twelve minutes if she cuts through the car park near the pharmacy, fourteen if she takes the long way past the church because sometimes Lukáš Fiala walks that route and she hasn't figured out if she wants to see him or not. School until 14:40 most days, Wednesdays until 13:50. Canteen lunch at 11:45 — the table near the bins with Tereza after October, before that she ate fast and left. After school: running three days a week with the school team at the sports complex on Pod Lázni, Karolína Dvořáková already there stretching when Anna arrived, never late, never early, annoying about it. Home by 17:00, shower that smelled like Babička's lavender soap, homework at the kitchen table while Babička cooked, Mum at work at the Tesco Express on Náměstí until 18:30. Evenings: Czech YouTube on her phone under the covers — Stejk, Jirka Král, whatever was recommended. Minecraft server with Matěj Král on Discord some nights, his mic always crackling like something was eating it. In bed by 21:30, not sleeping until 23:00, the church bells at 22:00 still wrong, still London-shaped in her head. Saturdays: Sainsbury's was gone but the café habit transferred — Tereza's mum's place on Náměstí, hot chocolate with the whipped cream that made a moustache, phones out, speaking in Czech-English that would've made Mrs. Navrátilová's eye twitch. Sundays: long run if there was a race coming, Babička's garden if there wasn't, the cherry tree dropping fruit that stained her fingers red and she didn't wash them off because the colour looked like she'd done something worth remembering.

key_scenes.location: ZŠ Benešov, Jiráskova, the girls' bathroom on the first floor, second stall from the window
key_scenes.what_happened: She felt it during Maths — Mrs. Navrátilová explaining something about fractions, Anna shifting in her seat, a wetness that wasn't right, wasn't normal, her stomach dropping faster than her brain could process. Made it to the bathroom. Locked the stall. Saw the stain. Red on blue uniform skirt, red on her underwear, red everywhere, and her hands were shaking and she had nothing, no pad, no tissue, nothing, and she was going to have to walk out like this and everyone would see and she'd be the English girl who bled through her skirt and that would be the new name, worse than angličanka, and she sat on the toilet lid and pressed her palms into her eyes and did not cry, would not cry, CRYING SHOULD BE LOUD OR IT DOESN'T COUNT and this wasn't loud, this was silent, this was drowning in a bathroom stall and then — footsteps. The door. Another stall opening. Then silence. Then something sliding under her door. A pad. Wrapped in that crinkly plastic. No note. No voice. No laugh. Just the pad and then the sink running and then the bathroom door closing and she was alone again with the thing that would save her and she didn't know who left it until she came out, washed her hands at the sink, saw the reflection of Tereza Bártová in the mirror — not looking at her, fixing her own hair, acting like nothing happened — and the recognition hit like the radiator knocking, like cause and effect, like a pattern her body understood before her mind did. Tereza didn't make it weird. Tereza didn't laugh. Tereza just helped and then walked back to class and Anna stood at that sink for three more minutes trying to figure out what to do with a person who was cruel and then wasn't.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Shock first. Then the specific shame of a body doing something without permission — the same feeling as biting Krish Patel, the same horror of being a passenger in yourself. Then gratitude so sharp it hurt, because she didn't want to owe Tereza Bártová anything, didn't want to be in debt to the laugh in the toilet, the angličanka. Then confusion, because the enemy just handed her the one thing she needed and walked away without asking for anything back, and that wasn't how enemies worked, that wasn't how anything worked, and the rules she'd built were wrong and the wrongness was terrifying and also, somewhere underneath the terror, a crack of light.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The crinkly plastic of the pad wrapper, the sound of it too loud in the bathroom silence, the way it felt like everyone in the school must be able to hear it even though no one was there

key_scenes.location: ZŠ Benešov, Jiráskova, the canteen, the table near the bins
key_scenes.what_happened: It wasn't a decision. Anna didn't walk over and ask to sit. She just — ended up there. Tray in hand, the canteen loud with the noise of two hundred kids eating, and her feet carried her past the empty table she usually sat at and toward the table near the bins where Tereza was already eating, and she sat down without asking and Tereza didn't look up and neither of them said anything for twenty minutes. Twenty minutes. She ate her svíčková. Tereza ate her řízek. The noise around them was enormous. The silence between them was enormous in a different way — not empty, not awkward, just present. Like the fish tank at Bright Horizons. Like Děda's chess games. Like being near someone who didn't need you to be anything. When Tereza finally spoke she said 'the sauce is shit today' and Anna said 'yeah' and that was it, that was the whole conversation, and they sat together the next day and the next and it became the thing they did without discussing why or how or what it meant, just the table near the bins, just the silence that said more than talking ever could.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Cautious. Like touching something that might bite. But also — relief. The specific relief of not having to perform, not having to explain, not having to translate herself into someone acceptable. Tereza had seen her bleeding and hadn't used it against her. That was the foundation. Everything else was built on a pad and a bathroom stall and twenty minutes of not-talking that meant more than any conversation she'd ever had.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The smell of the bins, slightly sweet and rotten, and how it became background noise after the first week, how she stopped noticing it the way you stop noticing the radiator knocking

key_scenes.location: Klára Liebigová's friend Jana's barbecue in the garden, some house on Mariánské náměstí that Anna had never been to before
who_was_there: various Czech adults she didn't know
key_scenes.what_happened: She grew three inches over summer and her chest changed and her face changed and she didn't notice it happening because you don't notice yourself from the outside, you just live in the body you have. But the barbecue — Mum's friend Jana, the one with the garden and the golden retriever — she walked in and Jana's face did something and then the words came out: 'Ta kráska!' The beauty. Like it was a gift. Like it was a prize Anna had won. And Mum's face did something else — pride, maybe, or fear, or that specific Czech mother thing where your daughter being pretty is both an achievement and a threat. And then the touching started — Jana's hand on Anna's shoulder, Jana's friend touching her hair, 'those cheekbones,' 'those eyes,' 'she'll break hearts' — and every touch felt like a claim, like they were marking territory on a body that belonged to them now, that was public property, that was a thing to be discussed while she stood there holding a plate of Jana's potato salad and wanting to dissolve. She didn't say anything. She ate the potato salad. She let them touch her hair. She went to the bathroom and looked at herself in the mirror and tried to see what they saw and couldn't, just saw Anna, just saw the same face she'd always had, except now it was apparently something other people thought they could comment on.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Skin crawling and powerful at the same time and hating herself for the powerful part. The attention was a spotlight and a cage and she couldn't separate them. Being called beautiful was supposed to be good — she knew that, she'd seen the movies, she'd watched the TikToks — but it felt like being told her body was no longer her own, that it had become a public thing, a thing that existed for other people to look at and touch and talk about. The worst part was the tiny warm feeling under the revulsion. The worst part was liking it. The worst part was being twelve and already understanding that being seen could be a kind of power and that the power came with a cost she hadn't agreed to.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The potato salad — too much mayo, little chunks of something crunchy, she ate the whole plate without tasting it because her mouth was busy not saying any of the things she wanted to say

key_scenes.location: The running track at the sports complex on Pod Lázni, a Thursday, she thinks, maybe October, maybe November
key_scenes.what_happened: The 1500m time trial. She'd been running since summer but this was the first one that mattered — the first one where her legs found the rhythm and her brain shut up and the radiator stopped knocking in her head and Mum's face stopped being tight and the angličanka stopped existing and there was just her feet and the track and the breath burning in her chest. Karolína was ahead for the first two laps and Anna didn't think about catching her, didn't think about anything, just ran, and then she was next to Karolína and then she was past her and then she crossed the line and her time was 5:23 and Karolína crossed three seconds later and they were both bent over with their hands on their knees and Anna's lungs were on fire and then Karolína put her hand on Anna's shoulder — just her hand, just briefly — and said 'dobrý, Liebigová' and Anna straightened up and Karolína was smiling, actually smiling, and then they hugged and it was the most sincere thing Anna had ever done with another human being and she didn't know why running made everything simple but it did, it just did, the rhythm drowning out the noise, the body becoming a thing that moved instead of a thing that was looked at.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Clean. For the first time in months she felt clean. Not the English girl, not the pretty one, not the container for Mum's grief, not the girl who owed Tereza Bártová a pad-debt — just a body moving through space, earning something instead of being given something. The hug was unexpected and sincere and she didn't know how to be sincere back so she just held on for one second longer than was normal and hoped Karolína understood.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The smell of the rubber track in cold weather, that specific burnt-rubber-and-frost smell, and how it meant her lungs were about to hurt and she loved the hurting because it was the only pain she chose

relationship_evolution.what_shifted: The bathroom rewired everything. Tereza was the laugh in the toilet, the angličanka, the face that found hers and held it too long — and then she was the hand that slid a pad under the door without being asked, without making it weird, without collecting the debt. The canteen silence sealed it. Twenty minutes of not-talking that said more than any apology could have. Anna didn't forgive Tereza — forgiveness felt too clean, too simple, like erasing something that should stay visible. But she let the cruelty and the kindness exist in the same person, and that was new, that was adult, that was the first time she understood that people could be more than one thing. The café on Náměstí became their country — the back corner, the free hot chocolate from Tereza's mum, the mixed Czech-English that was theirs and no one else's. Tereza never mentioned the bathroom again. Anna never mentioned the angličanka. The past existed between them like a scar — visible, permanent, but no longer bleeding.

She went from a name on the results board to the only person who understood what running was for. Not exercise. Not sport. Not achievement. Running was the off switch. Karolína got that without being told — she never asked why Anna ran, never asked about feelings, never tried to make it about anything other than splits and race strategies and the clean mathematics of distance over time. The hug after the time trial was the first physical contact Anna had initiated or received that didn't come with anxiety attached. It was sincere. She didn't know how to do sincere but she tried, and Karolína seemed to accept the attempt, and the friendship built itself in motion, in intervals, in the shared language of lactic acid and personal bests.

Mum noticed the body before Anna did. The comments started small — 'stand up straight, you're slouching,' 'that top is too tight,' 'boys are going to start looking at you' — and each one was a small invasion, a claim staked on territory that Anna hadn't mapped yet. Klára's anxiety had a new target: Anna's changing body, Anna's potential attractiveness, Anna's vulnerability to the male gaze that Klára understood better than Anna did. The anxious arms became the commenting arms, the critical arms, the arms that reached to pull her shirt down or fix her posture or warn her about things she didn't understand yet. Anna shrank from this attention the way she shrank from all of Mum's worry — by leaving the room, by running, by sitting in Babička's kitchen where nobody commented on bodies, they just put food in front of you and let you eat. The gratitude-cage of living with Mum got smaller. The love was still there. The suffocation was still there. Both things. Still.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Running as mental silence — the rhythm that drowns out the noise
knowledge_acquired.how: She ran because the school had a team and Babička said it would be good for her to do something with other kids. She kept running because the first time her legs found the pace — that specific pace where breathing becomes rhythm and thought becomes static — her brain shut up for the first time since she could remember. No radiator. No Mum's face. No angličanka. No ta kráska. Just feet and track and pain she chose. She didn't know other people used exercise this way. She didn't know it was a coping mechanism. She just knew that the half-hour after a hard run was the only time she felt like herself, not the version of herself other people saw.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Periods are not the end of the world but they are the end of something
knowledge_acquired.how: The bathroom stall. The pad under the door. The walk of shame back to class with a wad of toilet paper where dignity used to be. Nobody explained this would happen like THIS — Mum had told her about it, technically, in that halting Czech-English way where she couldn't look Anna in the eye, but the telling was clinical and the experience was visceral and the gap between the two was enormous. She learned that her body would do things without her permission. She learned that she would need to carry supplies like a soldier carries ammo. She learned that Tereza Bártová apparently always had a pad and that this fact said something about Tereza that Anna couldn't articulate yet.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Being looked at is a kind of theft
knowledge_acquired.how: The barbecue. Jana's hand on her shoulder. 'Ta kráska.' The touching. The comments. The sudden visibility. She didn't choose to grow three inches or to have cheekbones or to have whatever it was that made adults touch her hair like she was a showroom car. The looking felt like taking — like people were extracting something from her by looking, by commenting, by touching, and she was supposed to be grateful for the extraction because beauty was supposed to be a gift. She learned that the world thought her body was a thing it could discuss. She learned that she hated that. She learned that she also, secretly, in the smallest darkest corner of herself, liked it. She learned to hold both of those truths at the same time and not split open from the contradiction.

knowledge_acquired.thing: How to sit with someone in silence and have it mean something
knowledge_acquired.how: Tereza. The canteen. The table near the bins. Twenty minutes of not-talking that was louder than any conversation. She didn't learn this from anyone — it happened by accident, by not-having-anything-to-say and discovering that the not-saying was okay. That some people don't need you to perform. That silence can be a bridge instead of a wall. She didn't know the word 'companionable' but she understood the feeling, and she understood that the feeling was rare, and she understood that Tereza was the only person who'd ever given it to her outside of Děda's chess games and Babička's kitchen.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Being called pretty is not a compliment, it's a warning
opinion_snapshot.because: The barbecue. The hands in her hair. 'Ta kráska' like it was the only thing worth mentioning about her. She had a brain. She had a chess set she couldn't play. She had a running time that was getting faster. But what Jana and Mum's friends wanted to talk about was her face and her body and what she'd do to boys someday, and the conversation wasn't about her, it was about what she looked like, and that was a different thing entirely, and it made her feel like a thing instead of a person, and the warning was: this is what the world sees first, this is what matters to them, everything else is secondary

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Tereza Bártová is not the same person who laughed at her in the toilet
opinion_snapshot.because: The pad. The silence in the canteen. The way Tereza never mentioned the bathroom again, never used it against her, never collected the debt. People can be more than one thing. The girl who said angličanka and the girl who slid a pad under the door are the same person, and Anna doesn't have to forgive the first one to appreciate the second one, and the contradiction doesn't have to be resolved, it can just exist, like all the other contradictions she carries

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Running is the only time she's really herself
opinion_snapshot.because: The track. The rhythm. The way her brain shuts up when her legs are moving. When she runs she's not the English girl or the pretty one or the container for Mum's grief. She's just a body in motion, earning something, choosing the pain. The 1500m time trial — 5:23 — was the first number that belonged to her alone. Not given. Not inherited. Not commented on. Earned.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Mum's comments about her body are a new version of the anxious arms
opinion_snapshot.because: The posture comments. The clothes comments. The 'boys will look at you' comments. It's the same control dressed up as concern. Mum held her too tight as a baby and now she's trying to control how Anna presents to the world, and both things come from the same place — Klára's fear that something bad will happen, Klára's certainty that the world is dangerous, Klára's love that crushes because it doesn't know how to be light. The anxious arms became the commenting mouth. The grip just moved locations.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Bodies are traitors
opinion_snapshot.because: The period arriving without permission. The growing that happened while she wasn't paying attention. The bite that Krish Patel received before she decided to bite. The way her chest changed and suddenly people thought they could touch her hair. Bodies do things without asking. Bodies get noticed without consenting to be noticed. Bodies bleed and grow and betray you into visibility and you're supposed to be grateful because at least you're healthy, at least you're pretty, at least you're — no. The body is not hers. It's a rental. And the landlord keeps changing the terms.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: 'Ta kráska' — said with bitter irony to herself, never aloud, a curse disguised as a compliment
slang_and_language.origin: Jana at the barbecue, the hand on the shoulder, the smile that was supposed to be kind but felt like a brand. Anna took the phrase and made it a weapon she points at herself when she catches her reflection and feels the small warm feeling under the revulsion. Ta kráska. The beauty. The cage.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Mixed Czech-English with Tereza — 'that's so trap' next to 'to je hrozný,' switching mid-sentence without noticing
slang_and_language.origin: The café on Náměstí. The back corner. Hours of scrolling and talking and the two languages bleeding into each other because Tereza watched English TikTok and Anna thought in Czech now, mostly, and the intersection was a country only they lived in. 'Trap' from English slang meaning embarrassing — Tereza used it wrong and Anna didn't correct her because the wrongness was theirs.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Calling running 'the off switch' — never to anyone, just in her head
slang_and_language.origin: The first time her brain went quiet. The rhythm drowning the noise. She didn't have words for what running did so she made some up. The off switch. Like she was a machine that could be silenced. Like the radiator could stop knocking if she just ran fast enough.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: 'Dobrý' instead of 'good' or 'dobře' — the casual Czech approval that doesn't require the full word
slang_and_language.origin: Karolína Dvořáková after the time trial. 'Dobrý, Liebigová.' Not 'dobře' — that was what Děda said, that was careful, that was chess. 'Dobrý' was faster, looser, earned on a track instead of given across a board. Anna started using it for things that mattered — a good run, a good time, a good silence. Not for everything. Only for things that were actually good.

cringe_moments.moment: After the barbecue, after Jana called her ta kráska and touched her hair, she went to the bathroom and practised smiling at herself in the mirror — a pretty smile, a ta kráska smile, the kind of smile she thought she was supposed to have — and then Tereza's voice in her head said 'that's so trap' and she saw herself from the outside and it was the most embarrassing thing she'd ever done, standing in Jana's bathroom practising being pretty, and she couldn't unsee it, she still can't unsee it, the girl in the mirror learning to perform beauty for an audience that wasn't there
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because it revealed something she doesn't want to admit — that part of her wanted to be ta kráska, part of her wanted to be the kind of girl who smiles at herself and means it, and the wanting is embarrassing, the performing is embarrassing, the fact that she still checks herself in mirrors sometimes to see if she's pretty enough to justify the comments is the most embarrassing thing of all

cringe_moments.moment: Tereza's mum asked Anna what she wanted to be when she grew up and Anna said 'I don't know, pretty?' like it was a joke but it came out sincere and Tereza's mum did that Czech laugh that's not really a laugh and Anna wanted to crawl into the café's back corner and never come out, she'd meant it as a joke, she thinks she meant it as a joke, the fact that she isn't sure is the worst part
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because she doesn't know if she was joking and the uncertainty is a mirror she doesn't want to look into. Pretty isn't a career. Pretty isn't an identity. Pretty is a thing other people say about you. But for a second — just a second — it felt like the only honest answer.

cringe_moments.moment: Karolína Dvořáková asked why Anna always wore her running jacket zipped all the way up even when it wasn't cold and Anna said 'I like the weight' and then realized that was a weird thing to say and got red and Karolína just looked at her and said 'okay' and they never talked about it again but Anna knows Karolína noticed something and the being-noticed was worse than any comment about her body because Karolína wasn't looking at her body, she was looking at her brain, and the brain was easier to hide when the body was distracting
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because the running jacket is armor and she didn't mean to admit that armor existed and Karolína's 'okay' was the kind of okay that meant 'I see you' and being seen is the thing she fears most even though it's also the thing she wants most and the contradiction lives in that jacket, in the zipper she pulls up to her chin every morning, in the weight she pretends is just preference

outline.emotional_arc: From wanting someone who didn't want her to finding something she's actually good at — the first taste of belonging earned instead of given.
key_events: Ondřej Svoboda plays guitar in the school talent show, she can't stop staring, her chest does something she's never felt before
First crush hits her like a truck — she replays his performance, watches him in corridors, becomes consumed by wanting
Tries to flirt with Ondřej by doing the hair-flip thing she saw on TikTok — trips over her own bag, he doesn't notice the flip, just the trip, wants to dissolve into the floor
Ondřej asks Tereza out instead, Anna says 'good for you' and means the opposite, Tereza says no because of the girl code
Anna pretends she doesn't care, thinks about him for six more months, still feels stupid when she sees him in the corridor
Training intensifies — 4 times a week now, early mornings, the track becoming her second home
Středočeský kraj cross-country championship — she places 4th, the first time she felt like she belonged somewhere because of something she did, not something she was or looked like
The race bib goes on the bulletin board next to the London poster — evidence that she exists outside of other people's definitions
Matěj Král starts acting different around her since she 'got pretty' — stumbling over words, finding excuses to be near her, she pretends not to notice but misses how easy it used to be
The Discord calls with Matěj continue but there's a new awareness, a self-consciousness that wasn't there before, she doesn't know how to make it go away
new_or_changed_relationships: Ondřej Svoboda — former crush, now just someone she avoids eye contact with in the corridor, he has no idea, the humiliation lives in her chest like a splinter
Matěj Král — still close but weird now, the ease replaced by something loaded, he probably likes her and she doesn't know how to feel about that, misses the simplicity of before
Tereza Bártová — solidified as ride-or-die after the Ondřej situation, Tereza is the only one who knows about the crush, they speak in a mix of Czech and English nobody else can follow
outline.internal_state: Raw from rejection but energized by running. The Ondřej thing taught her that wanting is dangerous — you can want someone and they can want someone else and there's nothing you can do about it. Running is the opposite: you train, you race, you place. Cause and effect. The radiator knocked and heat came. She runs and she places. The world makes sense in motion.

deep_dive.daily_life_routines: Up at 5:45 on training days — Tuesdays, Thursdays, Saturdays, and the extra session Coach Novotný added on Wednesday mornings because she asked for it and he looked surprised she asked for anything. The alarm on her phone is the default iPhone chime and she hates it but changing it feels like admitting the early mornings bother her. Black tea with one sugar in Babička's kitchen, still dark outside, the radiator under the window knocking once when the heating kicks on and her shoulders going up before her brain catches up. School by 7:50 — ZŠ Benešov, Jiráskova, the same grey building, same smell of cabbage from the canteen, same walk with her earbuds in listening to the playlist she made for Ondřej that she's going to delete one day but hasn't yet. Wednesdays after school: the café on Náměstí with Tereza Bártová, hot chocolate Tereza's mum doesn't charge them for, the back corner booth where they sit for two hours scrolling TikTok and talking in the mix that nobody else can follow — 'that's so cringe, ale vážně, like proper cringe, šílený.' Fridays: no training, but she runs anyway, the field behind the school, circles until her legs stop feeling and her brain stops showing her Ondřej's hands on the guitar strings. Saturdays: long run with Karolína Dvořáková at the sports complex on Pod Lázni, then Babička's kitchen, then Discord with Matěj Král for three or four hours, Minecraft or just talking, but now there's this thing where she notices when he goes quiet and she didn't used to notice that. Sundays: Babička's house, the big lunch, trying not to watch the door when Mum leaves to meet Pavel Souček somewhere. Evening routine: homework at the kitchen table, the London poster above her bed, the race bib now pinned next to it, brushing her teeth, checking her phone, checking it again, putting it face-down so she stops checking it. Sleep at 10 if she's lucky. 3am if she's not. The 3am thoughts are always about Ondřej or Dad or the way Matěj said 'hezká' to his mum when he thought Anna couldn't hear. She doesn't know what to do with any of it so she does nothing and calls it fine.

key_scenes.location: ZŠ Benešov, Jiráskova, the school hall, the stage with the blue curtain
key_scenes.what_happened: The talent show. February, she thinks — maybe still cold outside, she remembers wearing her hoodie zipped to her chin. Ondřej walked on stage with an acoustic guitar that looked too big for him and played 'Riptide' by Vance Joy, the one that was everywhere that year, and his voice cracked on the high part in the second verse and he laughed, actually laughed, at himself, right there on stage, and the whole hall laughed with him but not at him, at the thing he'd done that was brave and imperfect and real. Anna's chest did something. Not butterflies — she'd call it butterflies later because that's what you're supposed to call it but it wasn't light and fluttery, it was heavy, like someone had dropped a stone in her stomach and the ripples were still moving. She couldn't stop staring at his hands. The way they moved on the strings. She'd never noticed hands before, not like this, not as something that could make you feel like the floor had tilted. Tereza elbowed her at some point and Anna realised her mouth was open and she closed it and didn't say anything and Tereza didn't ask and that was the beginning of the worst few months of her life so far.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Exposed. Like everyone could see what was happening to her. Like the thing in her chest was broadcasting on a frequency only she could hear but somehow everyone knew. She wanted to look away and couldn't. She wanted him to look at her and he didn't. He looked at the back of the hall, at the blue curtain, at his own hands. She was invisible and seen at the same time and the contradiction made her dizzy.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The blue stage light on the neck of the guitar and his fingers moving and the crack in his voice on the word 'melody' and the laugh that followed, that open-mouthed unembarrassed laugh, the sound of someone not afraid to fail in front of everyone.

key_scenes.location: ZŠ Benešov, Jiráskova, the corridor outside the main entrance, near the trophy case
who_was_there: some other kids she didn't register
key_scenes.what_happened: The hair-flip. March, probably, maybe April — she'd been watching TikToks about how to get a boy's attention for two weeks and she knew it was stupid, she KNEW, but she did it anyway. She saw him coming down the corridor — grey hoodie, the one with the small rip near the pocket, earbuds in, walking fast like he always did. She timed it. She actually timed it. The hair-flip, the over-the-shoulder thing she'd practised in the bathroom mirror until it looked natural, except her bag was on the floor because she'd taken her English book out and not put it back and her foot caught the strap and she went forward, not down, not completely, but enough. Stumbling. Arms out. The bag dragging. Her hair, which she'd flipped, now falling in her face. Ondřej took one earbud out, looked at her, said 'you alright?' in that flat Czech way that meant he didn't actually care about the answer, and kept walking. He didn't see the flip. He saw the trip. She stood there for maybe ten seconds with her back against the trophy case and her heart doing something violent and wrong and then she picked up her bag and went to the bathroom and sat in the second stall from the window and breathed until her hands stopped shaking.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: The specific horror of trying and failing publicly. Not the quiet rejection of not being noticed — that she could handle, that she was used to. This was the loud rejection of being noticed for the wrong thing. She'd performed femininity and it had gone wrong and everyone could see and Ondřej Svoboda would remember her as the girl who tripped over her own bag and nothing else. She wanted to crawl out of her skin and leave it there in the corridor.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The cold glass of the trophy case against her shoulder blade through her hoodie, and the strap of her bag cutting into her ankle, and the way his earbud dangled — the white cord, the small white bud — when he took it out to ask if she was alright.

key_scenes.location: The running track at the sports complex on Pod Lázni, October, she thinks a Thursday
who_was_there: runners from other schools across Středočeský kraj
key_scenes.what_happened: The championship. Cross-country, not track — the course wound through the fields behind the sports complex, 3.2 kilometres of mud and hills and cold October air that tasted like metal. She'd trained for this. Four times a week. Early mornings. The blisters she'd hidden, the shin splints she'd run through, the way she'd learned to breathe — in for three steps, out for two, a rhythm Děda would have recognised as patience. The start was chaos, elbows and spikes and someone's breath on her neck, and she held back like Coach Novotný told her to, held back until the hill at kilometre two and then she went, she just went, her legs finding something that wasn't effort anymore, it was just motion, just the body doing what she'd trained it to do. She came fourth. Fourth. Not first, not third, not on the podium, but the woman behind the table wrote her name on the results sheet and her name was there, Anna Liebigová, fourth place, Středočeský kraj cross-country championship, and nobody gave it to her and nobody could take it away. Karolína hugged her after — the second hug they'd ever shared, the first one that Anna returned properly, both arms, actual pressure. She didn't cry. She felt like crying. She didn't. The race bib went on the bulletin board above her bed, next to the London poster, and she looked at it every night before she slept and for the first time the looking didn't feel like missing something. It felt like having something.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Belonging that she'd earned. Not the passive belonging of being someone's daughter or someone's granddaughter or someone's foreign friend — the active belonging of having done a thing and the thing being real and measurable and hers. The radiator knocked and heat came. She trained and she placed. Cause and effect. The world making sense for the first time since she'd left it.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The mud on her shoes — she can still see it, the specific brown, the way it dried on the white mesh of her trainers — and the cold air going into her lungs like swallowing ice, and the sound of her own breathing louder than the crowd, in-three-out-two, in-three-out-two, the rhythm that replaced thinking.

relationship_evolution.what_shifted: From a boy in the corridor to the centre of her chest to a splinter she learned not to touch. He asked Tereza out in May, maybe June, she can't remember the exact day but she remembers the corridor and Tereza's face doing something complicated when he walked away and Tereza turning to her and saying 'I said no, obviously,' and Anna saying 'good for you' in a voice that didn't sound like hers, flat and Czech and wrong. The 'good for you' lives in her mouth like a rotten tooth. She meant 'thank you' and she meant 'I hate that you knew before I told you' and she meant 'I'm so embarrassed I could die' and she said 'good for you' because the other things were too big and too true. She still thinks about him. Not every day anymore. Most days. The wanting is quieter now but it's still there, under her ribs, and when she sees him in the corridor she looks at the floor or her phone or anywhere else because looking at him reminds her of the hair-flip and the trip and the way he said 'you alright?' like she was nothing.

The ease curdled. Not all at once — there was no moment, no conversation, no confession — just the slow accumulation of looks that lasted too long and silences where silences hadn't been before. He started complimenting her in March, small things, 'nice run today' becoming 'you looked fast out there' becoming 'hezká' said to his mum when he thought Anna couldn't hear, and each one landed on her like a insect she wanted to brush off but didn't know how. The Discord calls continued — Minecraft, school complaints, the same rhythm — but now she was aware of her own voice in a way she hadn't been before, checking her words, checking his tone, checking the silence for something she didn't want to name. She missed the simplicity. She missed being just a voice on a server, just blocks and jokes, just Matěj who never looked at her like that. She didn't know how to make it go back and she didn't know if she wanted it to go back and both answers felt wrong so she pretended nothing had changed and the pretending was its own kind of distance.

From closest friend to ride-or-die, which is a distinction that sounds the same but isn't. The Ondřej thing sealed it. Tereza said no to a boy she might have said yes to because Anna liked him, and that kind of loyalty doesn't come with instructions for gratitude, it just sits there, real and heavy, changing the weight of everything. They started speaking in the mix more — Czech and English tangled together, codeswitching mid-sentence, creating a language that was theirs and no one else's. 'He's such a dick, ale vlastně ne, he's just oblivious, like properly oblivious, úplně mimo.' Anna told Tereza about the hair-flip and Tereza laughed so hard she choked on her hot chocolate and then said 'that's the worst thing I've ever heard' with such genuine admiration that Anna almost felt okay about it. Almost. The friendship had a scar now — the angličanka, the bathroom — and the scar was visible and permanent but the tissue around it was stronger than before, and the Ondřej situation was the thing that proved it.

From training partner to the only person who understood what running was for. The championship changed something — the hug after the race, the way Karolína looked at her when the results went up, not surprised, not pitying, just nodding like of course, like she'd known Anna could do it before Anna knew it herself. They don't talk about feelings. They talk about splits and race strategy and which spikes are best for mud and whether Coach Novotný is going soft in his old age. But after the race there was something new — a respect that was mutual and unspoken, the recognition of someone who had seen her at her worst (gasping, leg cramps, the time she cried after a bad time trial and pretended it was allergies) and at her best, and both were real. Anna doesn't have many relationships like that. Straight. Clean. No hidden rooms.

knowledge_acquired.thing: How to want someone who doesn't want you — the specific anatomy of that, the way it lives in your chest and your stomach and the back of your throat at 3am
knowledge_acquired.how: Ondřej Svoboda. The talent show. The corridor. The hair-flip. The trip. The 'you alright?' The moment Tereza told her he'd asked her out and Anna's face did something she couldn't control and Tereza saw and said 'I said no, obviously.' Six months of wanting someone who didn't know she was alive except as the girl who tripped over her own bag. She learned that wanting doesn't create being-wanted. That attraction isn't reciprocal by default. That you can stare at someone's hands for weeks and they can look through you like glass. She learned it the hard way, which is the only way she ever learns anything.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Running as cause and effect — the clean mathematics of effort and result
knowledge_acquired.how: Training four times a week. Early mornings. The track becoming predictable, measurable, sane. Coach Novotný's split times written on the clipboard, the way the numbers went down week by week if she did the work. It was the opposite of everything else — the opposite of Ondřej, who she couldn't make look at her; the opposite of Dad, who she couldn't make stay; the opposite of Mum, who she couldn't make happy. Running gave her something none of those things could: the guarantee that if she put in the work, something would come back. The radiator knocked and heat came. She ran and she placed. The logic of it was the first logic she'd ever trusted.

knowledge_acquired.thing: How to spot when a boy likes you — and how to pretend you haven't noticed
knowledge_acquired.how: Matěj Král. The stumbling words. The way he found excuses to walk her to class. The 'hezká' said to his mum. The Discord calls where he went quiet in a way he never used to. She learned the signs not because anyone taught her but because the pattern was unmistakable once she saw it — the same pattern she'd performed with Ondřej, watching from corners, finding reasons to be near. Seeing it from the outside was different. Stranger. Uncomfortable. She didn't want Matěj to like her but she didn't know how to un-want it either, so she did what she always did: nothing, and called it fine.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Czech funeral customs — open casket, the specific silence, the flowers that smell too sweet
knowledge_acquired.how: Děda's funeral was last year but the knowledge persisted, changed shape, became something she carried into this year. The open casket. The way the dead look like themselves but wrong, like a painting where the colours are slightly off. The silence that isn't grief but politeness, the way Czech people stand in rows and don't say the thing they're thinking. She didn't cry at the funeral. She still doesn't know if that makes her strong or broken. She cried at 3am for two hours into her pillow and nobody saw and that felt right, that felt like the correct amount, the correct location, the correct audience of nobody.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Wanting is dangerous — it makes you visible and vulnerable and stupid
opinion_snapshot.because: Ondřej. The hair-flip. The trip. The 'you alright?' The six months of thinking about someone who never thought about her. She gave away the power the moment she wanted him and he never even knew she'd handed it over and that was the worst part — not the rejection but the invisibility of it, the fact that she'd been consumed by something he hadn't even noticed.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Running is the only thing that makes sense — the only place where effort produces result
opinion_snapshot.because: She trained four times a week and she placed fourth in the championship. Cause and effect. The radiator knocked and heat came. She ran and she placed. The world was chaotic and people were unreliable and wanting things made you weak but running was clean, running was math, running was the one system that hadn't failed her yet.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Being pretty is a trap — it makes people look at you but not see you
opinion_snapshot.because: The comments started this year — Mum's friends, strangers, boys at school. 'Stand up straight.' 'That top is too tight.' 'You could be a model.' Lukáš Fiala saying she was the prettiest girl in school like that was a compliment and not a cage. Matěj looking at her different since she 'got pretty' like she'd changed when she hadn't, like the outside had shifted and everyone decided the inside must have shifted too. Being pretty meant being looked at and being looked at meant being watched and being watched meant being held — not physically, but socially, trapped in the gaze, expected to perform the thing they saw when they looked at her.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Tereza Bártová is the only person who would say no to a boy for her and that means something she can't articulate
opinion_snapshot.because: Girl code. The stupid phrase that shouldn't mean anything but means everything. Tereza could have said yes. Tereza probably wanted to say yes — Anna saw her face when Ondřej walked away, the brief flicker of temptation before the choice locked in. And Tereza chose Anna. Chose loyalty over opportunity. Anna has never been chosen first before. Not by Dad, who chose Siân and the flat in Croydon. Not by Mum, who chose to move them here. Not by anyone, ever, in any way that didn't come with conditions or guilt or the weight of 'I did this for you.' Tereza's 'no' was clean. No debt. No guilt. Just the choice. Anna doesn't know how to thank someone for that so she doesn't try, she just shows up at the café every Wednesday and hopes Tereza understands.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Mixed Czech-English with Tereza — 'that's so cringe, ale vážně, like proper cringe, šílený'
slang_and_language.origin: Tereza Bártová watches English TikTok, Anna grew up with English, the combination became their private language, nobody else at school can follow it and that's the point, it's a border they control, a country with two citizens

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: 'Good for you' — said flat, said Czech, said wrong
slang_and_language.origin: What she said when Tereza told her Ondřej had asked her out. She meant 'thank you for saying no' and she meant 'I hate this' and she meant 'I'm dying' and she said 'good for you' because the other things were too honest and honesty felt like bleeding in public. The phrase has calcified. She says it now when she means the opposite. Tereza knows. That's why it works.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Running terminology used as emotional shorthand — 'splits,' 'personal best,' 'lactic acid'
slang_and_language.origin: From Coach Novotný, from Karolína Dvořáková, from the language of the track. When she can't describe how she feels about Ondřej she says 'bad split.' When something goes right she says 'personal best.' The metaphor is unconscious. She doesn't know she's doing it.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: 'Proboha' — still using it, still Czech, still the word that comes out when she bumps into things or drops something or sees something that makes her stomach drop
slang_and_language.origin: Klára. The fridge. The word that lives in her body before it lives in her mind. She said it once at school and someone laughed and she felt the old shame from when Markéta Dvořáková explained what it meant, but she can't stop saying it, it's not a choice, it's a reflex, it's the sound her mouth makes when the world goes wrong.

cringe_moments.moment: The hair-flip. The trip. Ondřej saying 'you alright?' and walking away. The whole corridor seeing. The bag. The strap. The way her hair fell in her face instead of over her shoulder like the TikTok girls. She went to the bathroom after and sat in the second stall and pressed her forehead against the door and the door was cold and she deserved the cold because she had tried to be something she wasn't and the world had corrected her, publicly, painfully, specifically.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because he didn't see the flip. He only saw the trip. Because she'd practised in the mirror like a performing seal and the performance failed and the failure was witnessed by the one person she wanted to witness the success. Because 'you alright?' is what you say to a stranger who falls, not to someone you notice, and his voice was flat and his eyes were already moving on and she was already behind him in the corridor, already in his past, already forgotten.

cringe_moments.moment: Saying 'good for you' to Tereza about Ondřej asking her out. The flat voice. The Czech accent thickening on the English words because she couldn't control it, because the feelings were too big and the accent was what happened when feelings were too big. Tereza's face — not fooled, never fooled, seeing right through her. The silence after. The shame of being known.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because she couldn't say thank you and she couldn't say I'm devastated and she couldn't say anything true so she said something false in a voice that made the falseness obvious. It was the opposite of crying loud. It was crying silent and being seen anyway and being seen was the thing she was trying to avoid.

cringe_moments.moment: Watching Ondřej's hands during the talent show and Tereza elbowing her and realising her mouth was open. Actually open. Like a fish. Like someone who'd never seen a boy before. Like someone who couldn't control her own face. In public. In the school hall. In front of everyone.
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because Tereza noticed. Because noticing meant knowing. Because knowing meant Tereza could see inside her chest to the thing that was happening there and Anna hadn't given permission for that. The openness of her own face felt like a betrayal — her body doing something again without her consent, like biting Krish Patel, like crying at 3am, like all the things she couldn't control.

outline.emotional_arc: From resistance to a man who isn't her father to the slow realization that she's becoming someone even she doesn't fully recognize.
key_events: Mum starts dating Pavel Souček — a guy from the building supply shop on the main road, quiet, polite, tries too hard
Anna hates him immediately and without reason — he's not her dad, he's not anything, he's just there taking up space that shouldn't be filled
Pavel tries to connect — buys her trainers, asks about school, attempts conversation — Anna gives one-word answers and feels the betrayal in her own politeness
The 'Dad' slip at dinner — accidentally called Pavel 'Dad,' everyone went silent, she said she meant nothing by it, she meant everything by it, she'll never recover
Fights with Mum escalate — Klára says Anna is being unfair, Anna says Klára didn't give her a vote, both are right, neither cares
Pavel starts staying over, Anna feels the house contract, the walls closer, another person in the space that was supposed to be safe
Wore the trainers Pavel bought exactly once to shut Mum up, felt like a traitor, never wore them again
Running becomes more urgent — the track is the only place where the house isn't following her, where Pavel doesn't exist, where she isn't someone's daughter or someone's problem
The fights with Klára get meaner — 'You chose this life, I didn't,' Anna says, and knows she's being unfair, and doesn't care
Babička's kitchen remains neutral territory — no Pavel, no fighting, just koláče and old Czech TV and the safety of a woman who expects nothing
new_or_changed_relationships: Pavel Souček — the intruder, the man who isn't her father, his presence feels like a rewrite of a story she didn't consent to, every kindness is a trap
Klára Liebigová — fighting more now, the gratitude cage tightening, Anna knows she's supposed to be grateful but gratitude feels like surrender
Babička Marie Novotná — still the safest person, the only one who doesn't want anything from Anna, the kitchen that doesn't ask questions
outline.internal_state: Angry in a way she can't name. The house isn't hers anymore — Pavel's coat on the hook, Pavel's mug in the cupboard, Pavel's car in the driveway. She's being erased and rewritten at the same time and no one asked her permission. The 'Dad' slip lives in her chest like a wound. She didn't mean it. She meant all of it.

deep_dive.daily_life_routines: Wake up at 6:15 because Babička's floorboards creak and that's earlier than the alarm. Lie there counting the sounds — Pavel's car isn't in the driveway yet, good, the house is still hers for twenty more minutes. Shower. The hot water takes forever because the boiler is old and Babička won't let anyone touch it. School by 7:40, the walk down Na Purkrabství past the building supply shop where Pavel works, she speeds up when she passes it, doesn't look at the sign, Mravní stavba or whatever it's called. Classes. English with Mr. Procházka who asks her to help with pronunciation and she wants to scream 'I'm not your teaching assistant.' Lunch in the canteen — the boiled cabbage smell has become background noise, she doesn't notice it anymore. After school: the track.
