Coach Novotný (no relation, everyone asks) does intervals on Tuesdays and Thursdays, long runs on Wednesdays, rest or easy jog on the other days. The track is the only place where the house isn't following her. Home around 5 or 6 depending. If Pavel's grey Škoda is in the driveway, she goes to Babička's kitchen instead. Sits. Eats whatever's there — koláče, chlebíčky, sometimes just bread and butter if Babička hasn't baked. Babička doesn't ask about school. Babička doesn't ask about Pavel. Babička just puts food down and hums and it's the only quiet left. If Pavel's not there, she goes upstairs to the attic room, lies on the bed, stares at the London poster, scrolls her phone. Tereza sends voice notes — the mix, Czech and English tangled up, 'guess who was being a complete prick today' — and Anna replies but shorter than she used to. Dinner is the minefield. Pavel started staying over in January, maybe February, she can't remember exactly, and now there's a fourth plate on the table and his coat on the hook by the door and his shoes in the hallway and he takes up space like he's always been there and he hasn't, he hasn't, the house was full before him, it didn't need filling. She eats fast and leaves. Goes to bed. Doesn't sleep. The 3am hours are the worst — that's when the anger has nowhere to go and it just sits on her chest like something heavy and warm and wrong. Running at 3am isn't an option so she lies there and listens to the house settle and tries not to think about the word that came out of her mouth at dinner that one time. Weekends: Saturday morning with Mum used to be Sainsbury's and the café and the magazine. Now Saturday morning is sometimes Pavel making eggs in Babička's kitchen and Anna leaving without eating. The track on Saturday afternoon. Tereza on Saturday evening, the café on Náměstí, free hot chocolate, the back corner where they sit for hours. Sunday — Dad used to be Sunday. Now Sunday is FaceTime with Dad if he remembers, and he usually remembers, and it's always 'alright, Munch?' and she always says 'fine' and it's always a lie and she doesn't care anymore if he can tell.

key_scenes.location: Babička's kitchen on Na Purkrabství, the wooden table by the window
key_scenes.what_happened: The dinner. It was a Thursday, she thinks. Maybe a Wednesday. Pavel had brought some kind of cake from the bakery on the square — not Babička's koláče, something else, something with cream that was too sweet and wrong. He put it on the table like an offering and sat down and everyone was being so nice, Klára's face was doing that soft thing it does when Pavel's around, Babička was pouring tea, and Anna was eating the cake because it was there and she was hungry and it tasted like nothing. And then she needed the butter, which was next to Pavel, and she opened her mouth and what came out was 'Dad, can you pass the butter.' The word was in the room before she could stop it. Dad. Not Pane. Not Pavel. Dad. Like it was the most natural thing in the world. Like she'd been saying it forever. The silence was physical. Klára's fork stopped mid-air. Babička's hand went still on the teapot. Pavel's face did something complicated — hope and fear and something like tenderness — and Anna wanted to scrape the word back into her mouth and swallow it. 'I meant nothing by it,' she said. Her voice was flat. Czech. Wrong. 'Just a word.' She took the butter from his hand without looking at him. She didn't eat the rest of the cake. She went upstairs and didn't come down again. The word lived in her chest like something sharp and she couldn't pull it out because pulling it out would mean admitting it was there.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Horror. Not at the slip itself — at the part of her that had wanted to say it. At the part that was tired of fighting. At the part that thought 'what if I just let this be easy.' The betrayal was double: betraying Dad by saying it, betraying herself by meaning it, even a little, even for a second, even just because she was tired and the butter was right there and he was right there and sometimes it would be easier to just let someone else be the father-shaped thing in the room. The self-hatred was immediate and total. She wanted to peel her own skin off. She wanted to go back in time thirty seconds. She wanted to never have been hungry.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The texture of the cake — wet, cloying, cream that stuck to the roof of her mouth like the word stuck in the air

key_scenes.location: The running track at the sports complex on Pod Lázni, a Thursday, she thinks, maybe November, maybe December
key_scenes.what_happened: Intervals. 400s. Coach Novotný said eight, she did ten because the house was still in her lungs and ten wasn't enough to get it out. Karolína Dvořáková was on the track too, doing her own workout, and they didn't talk because they don't talk during sessions, that's not what the track is for. But on the tenth 400, Anna's legs were screaming and her lungs were on fire and she looked over at Karolína and Karolína looked back and something passed between them — recognition, maybe, or just the shared knowledge of what it means to run until the thing you're running from can't keep up. After, bent over hands-on-knees, Karolína said 'good session' and Anna said 'yeah' and that was the whole conversation and it was perfect. No questions. No 'are you okay.' No 'your mum called.' Just the track and the time and the clean mathematics of distance over pace. She sat on the curb afterward and drank water and looked at the grey sky and the grey building and the grey November and thought: this is the only place I'm real. Not someone's daughter. Not someone's problem. Not the girl who said Dad to the wrong man. Just a body moving through space at a measurable speed.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Clean. The only clean feeling left. The anger went somewhere during the tenth interval — not away, but down, into the legs, into the ground, into the lactic acid that was burning through her calves. Running was the off switch and she needed it more now than she'd ever needed it before. Pavel didn't exist on the track. Klára's tight face didn't exist. The 'Dad' slip didn't exist. Just splits. Just breath. Just the raw animal fact of a body that could still move.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The metallic taste of effort at the back of her throat, like licking a coin, like the air was solid and she had to chew through it

key_scenes.location: Babička's house on Na Purkrabství, the hallway by the front door
key_scenes.what_happened: The fight. Not the first one, not the last one, but the one that drew blood. It started because Anna came home from school and Pavel was in the kitchen again, making tea, wearing slippers — slippers! — like he lived there, and Anna walked past without saying anything and Klára followed her into the hallway and said 'you could at least say hello' and Anna said 'why' and Klára said 'because he's trying, Anna, he's trying and you won't even —' and Anna said 'I didn't choose this.' The words came out fast and mean and Czech and she didn't plan them but she didn't stop them either. 'You chose this life, I didn't.' Klára's face did the thing — the tight mouth, the held breath, the wounded-animal look that Anna knows is real but can't respond to anymore because responding to it means making herself smaller and she's been small her whole life. 'I'm doing this for us,' Klára said. 'You're doing this for you,' Anna said. The silence after was worse than the shouting. Klára went back to the kitchen. Anna went upstairs. Neither of them apologised. The fight lived in the house for days afterward, in the way they didn't look at each other, in the way Klára set Anna's plate down harder than necessary, in the way Anna said 'dobrou' at bedtime like it was a weapon. They were both right. Neither of them cared.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Righteous and wrong at the same time. The anger was clean for about five seconds — the relief of finally saying it, of naming the thing she'd been swallowing — and then it curdled into guilt because Klára's face was Klára's face, the same face that cried in the car at nursery, the same face that bumped the fridge every day, the same face that was doing the best it could with a life it hadn't planned either. But the guilt didn't make the anger go away. They just sat next to each other, heavy and hot, and Anna couldn't hold both at once so she held the anger because the anger was hers and the guilt was Mum's and she was tired of carrying things that belonged to other people.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: Pavel's slippers — brown, worn at the heel, sitting by the door like they had every right to be there

relationship_evolution.what_shifted: He went from invisible to intolerable the moment his coat appeared on the hook by the door. Before that he was just Mum's friend, just a name, just the guy from Mravní stavba who sometimes appeared at Babička's house with flowers that Anna ignored. But the coat changed everything. The coat meant staying. The coat meant his smell in the hallway, his shoes next to hers, his presence in the kitchen in the morning when she wanted it to be empty. He tried — God, he tried, with the trainers and the questions about school and the way he said 'dobrý den' every morning like he was asking permission to exist — and every attempt made it worse because every attempt was proof that he was trying to be something he wasn't, couldn't be, shouldn't be. The 'Dad' slip destroyed something. Not the relationship — there was no relationship to destroy — but the possibility of one, the tiny stupid part of her that had almost, for one second, felt something like ease. Now she couldn't look at him without seeing the hope on his face when the word came out, and the hope was worse than anything because hope meant he wanted it and she didn't want him to want it, she wanted him to stay in his lane, to be Mum's boyfriend and nothing more, to not try to be family because family was already full, family was already broken, there was no room.

The gratitude cage got smaller. Every time Klára defended Pavel, every time she said 'he's trying,' every time she asked Anna to be polite, the bars closed in another inch. Anna knew the script — Mum sacrificed everything, Mum left her country, Mum is doing her best — but knowing the script didn't make her want to perform it. The fights got meaner because the truth got closer. 'You chose this life, I didn't' — that was the blade, and she used it, and she knew she was being unfair, and the unfairness didn't stop her because unfair was the only weapon she had against a woman who had already won by being her mother. The love was still there. The suffocation was still there. But now there was something new — the recognition that Klára had been Anna's age once, had been a girl with a mother who was probably anxious too, had grown up and chosen a man and a country and lost both and was choosing again, and the choosing was brave or stupid or both, and Anna couldn't decide if she respected it or hated it.

Babička became the only room left. The kitchen didn't ask questions. The kitchen didn't defend Pavel. The kitchen just put food down and hummed and let Anna sit in silence for as long as she needed. Anna started going there more — not just after school but before school, during fights, during the evenings when Pavel's car was in the driveway and the house felt like it was shrinking. Babička never said 'you should be nicer to him.' Babička never said 'your mother deserves happiness.' Babička just made koláče and watched her old Czech TV shows and let the world be what it was. This neutrality was the only safe thing and Anna clung to it without admitting she was clinging. She didn't know that Babička had been Klára's age once too, had made choices too, had probably felt the same cage of gratitude and resentment. She didn't know that Babička's silence was a choice, not an absence. She just knew the kitchen was warm and nobody asked her to be anyone she wasn't.

He became the ghost in the room when Pavel was there. Every kindness from Pavel was a reminder that Dad wasn't providing kindnesses anymore — not in person, not in the same way, not with the same ease. The FaceTime calls were shorter now. 'How's school?' 'Fine.' 'Mum doing alright?' 'Yeah.' The rhythm was a skeleton, bones without meat. She didn't tell him about Pavel. She didn't know why. Maybe because saying it would make it real, would mean England was really gone, would mean the flat above Ladbrokes was really someone else's life now. Or maybe because she didn't want him to feel replaced, didn't want to give him that power, didn't want to admit that there was a space that could be filled by someone who wasn't him. The 'Dad' slip lived in her like a secret she was keeping from both of them — from Pavel because he didn't deserve it, from Marcus because he didn't earn it. She didn't know which betrayal was worse.

knowledge_acquired.thing: How to make a house feel smaller by adding one person
knowledge_acquired.how: Pavel's coat on the hook. Pavel's mug in the cupboard — the blue one with the chip on the rim, she hates that mug, it's always in the sink. Pavel's car in the driveway taking up the space where her running shoes used to dry. Pavel's smell — deodorant and something woody, not Dad's smell, never Dad's smell — in the hallway when she comes downstairs. The house didn't change size. The house just started pressing in.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Politeness is its own kind of violence
knowledge_acquired.how: She said 'dobrý den' to Pavel every morning because Mum asked her to and the word tasted like giving in. She said 'děkuji' when he bought the trainers because not saying it would have been a scene and she didn't want to give him the scene. Every polite word was a small surrender and she felt each one in her chest, a tiny betrayal of the part of her that wanted to scream.

knowledge_acquired.thing: The body keeps running when the mind wants to stop
knowledge_acquired.how: The tenth 400. The legs that wouldn't quit. The lungs that burned and kept breathing. She learned that she could outrun the anger, at least temporarily, at least for the forty-five minutes of a hard session, and the outrunning was the closest thing to peace she had. She didn't know this was a coping mechanism. She just knew that the track was the only place where the house wasn't.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Guilt doesn't cancel anger — they just stack
knowledge_acquired.how: She felt guilty about the fights with Mum and angry about feeling guilty and guilty about being angry and the whole thing was a spiral she couldn't find the top of. Nobody taught her how to hold two feelings at once. Nobody taught her that the love and the suffocation could both be true. She just held them and they were heavy and she couldn't put either one down.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Pavel Souček is not her father and never will be
opinion_snapshot.because: He wears slippers in someone else's house. He buys trainers like affection can be purchased. He says 'dobrý den' every morning like he's asking permission to exist in a space that was never his. He's not Dad. He's not anything. He's just there, taking up room, making the house smaller, making Mum's face go soft in a way it never went for Dad, and the softness is worse than the anger because it means Mum has moved on and Anna hasn't and the gap between them is growing.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: The house isn't hers anymore
opinion_snapshot.because: The coat on the hook. The mug in the sink. The slippers by the door. The car in the driveway. Every object is a flag planted in territory she thought was safe. Babička's house was supposed to be neutral — not England, not the flat above Ladbrokes, not anywhere that belonged to anyone — and now it belongs to Pavel too, slowly, one object at a time, and she can feel herself being pushed out of rooms she used to sit in.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Gratitude is a trap
opinion_snapshot.because: Mum says 'I'm doing this for us' and the 'us' is supposed to include Anna but it doesn't feel like inclusion, it feels like conscription. She didn't vote for this. She didn't choose the move or the country or the man. She's supposed to be grateful for a life she didn't pick and the gratitude is a cage with nice bars — you can see through them but you can't get out. Saying thank you for the trainers was saying 'okay, you're allowed to be here' and she doesn't think he's allowed to be here but she said it anyway because the alternative was a fight she was too tired to have.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Running is the only thing that's actually hers
opinion_snapshot.because: The track doesn't have a fourth place setting. Coach Novotný doesn't ask her to be polite. The intervals don't care if she's grateful. Running is the only place where she isn't someone's daughter or someone's problem or someone's obligation. It's just a body moving through space at a measurable speed and the measurability is the only honest thing left.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Mum is happier and that's worse somehow
opinion_snapshot.because: The tight face is looser. The 'proboha' comes less often. Klára hums in the kitchen now, actual humming, not the anxious kind. This should be good. Anna knows it should be good. But it isn't good because the happiness has a source and the source is Pavel and Pavel is the problem and how can the problem and the solution be the same person? She can't be happy for her mother without being angry at the cause of the happiness and the contradiction is exhausting and she doesn't know how to hold it.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Calling Pavel 'on' — the Czech formal 'he' — instead of by name
slang_and_language.origin: Started in January when he began staying over. She refused to say his name in the house because naming him would make him real, would make him permanent. 'On' was distance. 'On' was a wall built out of a pronoun. Klára noticed. Klára said 'you could call him Pavel.' Anna said nothing and kept saying 'on.'

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Mixed Czech-English insults — 'he's such a dick, ale vlastně ne, he's just oblivious'
slang_and_language.origin: With Tereza Bártová only. The mix is theirs, a language that belongs to no country, and it's the only way she can talk about Pavel without the words feeling like they belong to him. In English he's 'a dick.' In Czech he's 'on.' Between the two languages there's a space where she can say what she actually means.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: 'Proboha' said at Pavel instead of about the fridge
slang_and_language.origin: The word used to be Mum's, used to belong to the bumping and the anxiety and the small daily frustrations. Now Anna says it under her breath when Pavel's car pulls into the driveway. She doesn't know she's using her mother's language. She doesn't know the word is migrating, becoming hers, becoming the sound she makes when the world is too close.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'fine' to Dad on FaceTime — one word, flat, a door closing
slang_and_language.origin: Used to be 'alright, Munch?' 'Yeah, good, Dad, how are you?' Now it's 'alright, Munch?' 'Fine.' The word is a wall. The word is the distance between Croydon and Benešov made audible. She doesn't know she's doing it. She doesn't know he can hear the gap growing.

cringe_moments.moment: The 'Dad' slip at dinner — calling Pavel 'Dad' when asking for the butter
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because she didn't mean it and she meant it and the part that meant it is the part she can't forgive. Because his face did the hope thing and the hope was worse than contempt would have been. Because Mum heard it and Babička heard it and the word was in the room like a smell that wouldn't leave. Because she said 'I meant nothing by it' and her voice was flat and lying and everyone knew it was lying and nobody said anything about the lie. Because she replayed it every night for months, lying in the attic room, the word sitting on her chest, the butter sitting on the table, the hope on his face sitting in her memory like something that wouldn't dissolve. She will never recover from this. She knows this the way she knows the radiator is alive — without evidence, without logic, with the absolute certainty of a body that has learned something the mind hasn't accepted yet.

cringe_moments.moment: Wearing the trainers Pavel bought — the white Nikes with the pink swoosh, exactly once, to shut Mum up
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because she looked at her feet in those trainers and saw herself being bought. Because she said 'děkuji' and meant 'I hate this' and the word came out polite and the politeness was the betrayal. Because she wore them to school and Tereza said 'nice shoes' and Anna said 'mum's boyfriend bought them' and the way she said 'boyfriend' was a weapon and she knew it and used it anyway. Because she took them off when she got home and put them in the back of the wardrobe and never wore them again and every time she sees them there — white and pink and clean and unworn — she feels the shape of a gratitude she doesn't actually have and the absence of it makes her feel like a fraud.

cringe_moments.moment: The time she slammed the door harder than she meant to and Babička's picture fell off the wall
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: The frame cracked. The glass didn't break but the corner of the frame split and it was a photo of Děda František, young, standing in front of the house, and the crack went right through his chest. She picked it up and her hands were shaking and she wanted to say sorry but sorry wasn't big enough so she said nothing and put the photo face-down on Babička's table and walked out. Babička never mentioned it. Babička fixed the frame herself. Anna saw it back on the wall a week later with glue in the crack, visible if you looked. She looked. She always looked. The crack was her fault and the glue was Babička's forgiveness and neither of them talked about it and that was worse somehow than if Babička had shouted.

outline.emotional_arc: From facing the life her father built without her to the terrifying realization that being wanted is its own kind of trap.
key_events: Visits Dad in London summer of 2023 — his flat in Croydon, smaller than she expected, cleaner than their flat above Ladbrokes ever was
Meets Siân for the first time — she's not tall and mean like Anna imagined, she's short and tries too hard and has a nervous laugh, Anna doesn't know what to do with this
Meets Poppy — her half-sister, three years old, blue eyes like Dad, Anna looks at her and feels nothing on purpose because feeling something would crack her open
Dad says 'alright, Munch?' and it sounds the same and completely different, the performance of familiarity, the effort visible at the edges
The flat has no radiator that knocks, no damp spot, no spare room — it's a different life, a cleaner life, a life she wasn't invited to until now
Dad asks if she's happy in Czech Republic, she says 'yeah,' the lie comes easy, the truth would break something she can't afford to break
Comes back to Benešov and everything looks grey, the house looks smaller, she hates that she noticed
Lukáš Fiala tells her she's the prettiest girl in school — she freezes, says 'okay,' walks away, replays it every night for a month hating herself
The voice note disaster — sent a voice note in English to Tereza complaining about her but sent it to a class group chat by mistake, had to leave the group, Tereza found it funny, Anna did not
Dad asks if she wants to visit for Christmas, she says she's busy, she wasn't, the lie is easier than the truth
FaceTime calls with Dad become even more stilted — he's trying, she can see him trying, but trying isn't the same as being there
new_or_changed_relationships: Marcus Liebig — the visit made the distance real in a new way, he has a whole life that doesn't include her, the flat is evidence, Poppy is evidence, Siân is evidence
Siân Liebig — no longer imaginary, no longer the villain, just a nervous woman trying too hard, this is worse than the villain version
Poppy Liebig — the half-sister she doesn't know how to feel about, the proof that Dad kept building while she was being rebuilt somewhere else
Lukáš Fiala — a boy who called her pretty and got 'okay' for his trouble, she thinks about him at 3am regularly, doesn't know what to do with nice
outline.internal_state: Cracked open by London, by Poppy, by the clean flat without a knocking radiator. The nothing she felt on purpose was protection but protection doesn't last — the cracks let things in. Lukáš Fiala said she was pretty and she froze because pretty is what Mum's friends say, pretty is what strangers notice, pretty is the thing that isn't her. She wants to be fast, not beautiful. She wants to be chosen for what she does, not how she looks. But the boy said she was pretty and she can't stop thinking about it.

deep_dive.daily_life_routines: September onwards and the routine is: wake at 6:15 before Babička's floorboards creak, lie in bed staring at the London poster until the guilt or the restlessness kicks her upright. Running kit on by 6:40, out the door by 6:50, down Na Purkrabství past the houses with their shutters still closed, through the park to the track at Pod Lázni. Forty minutes, sometimes fifty if her legs have that empty feeling that needs burning out. Back by 7:40, shower that never gets hot enough, breakfast at Babička's table — rohlíky with butter, sometimes jam if Babička's already been to the bakery, never talking because Babička doesn't ask questions at breakfast and that's the whole point. School at Gymnázium Benešov by 8:20, sit in the middle now, not the back, not since Mrs. Navrátilová, Mr. Procházka's English class on Tuesdays and Thursdays where she mouths the words correctly before he says them and hates herself for showing off without meaning to. Lunch in the canteen with Tereza Bártová and Karolína Dvořáková, the table by the window not the one near the bins anymore, they've moved up in the world or at least away from the smell. After school: the café on Náměstí if Tereza's mum is working, free hot chocolate and scrolling TikTok and the mixed language that's become their default. Home by five, homework at the kitchen table while Babička cooks, the sound of Czech television from the sitting room. Dinner at six, always soup first, always meat and dumplings second, always Babička watching to make sure she's eating enough. Evening: the attic room, the phone, the group chat she got kicked out of and re-added to after the voice note thing, Discord with Matěj Král if he's online but it's weird now so sometimes she just watches Czech YouTube instead — Kamila Pokorná's cooking channel, Martin Kyšperský interviews, things that don't require her to be anyone. Bed at ten, sleep at eleven, awake at three at least twice a week thinking about Lukáš Fiala or Poppy's blue eyes or the flat in Croydon that didn't have a knocking radiator. Weekends: Saturday with Mum, the Sainsbury's routine replaced by the Tesco routine, different shop same script, the magazine, the café, the walking home. Sunday: long run, sometimes with Karolína, sometimes alone, the field behind the school, the path near Konopiště, wherever her legs take her. The Sunday run is church. The Sunday run is the only thing that doesn't remind her of anywhere else.

key_scenes.location: Marcus Liebig and Siân Liebig's flat on Lebanon Road, Croydon, the sitting room
key_scenes.what_happened: The flat was smaller than the flat above Ladbrokes and cleaner than the flat above Ladbrokes and the absence of the knocking radiator was so loud she could hear it — the silence where the fist should be. Siân opened the door and she was short, shorter than Mum, with a nervous laugh that came out three times in the first thirty seconds and a top that had a small coffee stain on the cuff that she kept touching. 'You must be Anna, we've heard so much about you' — the phrase was so English it hurt, so performed, and Anna said 'ahoj' instead of hello because the Czech came out when the English wouldn't. Dad appeared behind Siân with Poppy on his hip and said 'alright, Munch?' and the word was the same but his voice was different, softer, the voice you use with a toddler still in the room, and Anna saw him adjust — saw him try to be both fathers at once, the one who gave her McVitie's and the one who gives Poppy whatever Poppy gets. Poppy had blue eyes. His blue eyes. She looked at this small person who had his eyes and his last name and his Sunday mornings now and she felt nothing, chose nothing, built a wall out of nothing and stood behind it. The flat had no damp spot. The bathroom had no curling wallpaper. The spare room was a nursery with a cot and a mobile with felt clouds. She stood in the doorway of the nursery for ten seconds that lasted a year and then Siân said 'would you like some tea?' and Anna said 'yeah, okay' in English because English was what this flat spoke and she could still speak it, she could still pass, she could still pretend the language hadn't forked inside her.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: The nothing was a performance. Underneath: fury at the clean walls, grief for the knocking radiator she never thought she'd miss, jealousy so sharp it tasted like metal, and the horrible recognition that Siân was trying, actually trying, and a nervous woman trying too hard was worse than a villain because villains you can hate and nervous women you can only feel guilty about hating.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The coffee stain on Siân's cuff — she kept touching it, rolling the fabric between her fingers, and Anna could see the small brown mark and thought 'she's nervous too' and the thought was unbearable because it meant Siân was a person and not a force.

key_scenes.location: The corridor outside the main entrance at Gymnázium Benešov, near the trophy case
key_scenes.what_happened: September, maybe October, she can't remember exactly, a Thursday maybe, she was walking to the exit with her bag over one shoulder and Tereza's voice note playing through her earbud when Lukáš Fiala appeared from the side corridor like he'd been waiting. He had his sports bag and his hair was still damp from the shower after football training and he said 'hey, Anna' and she pulled out one earbud and said 'what' in Czech, not mean, just flat, the way she talks to boys she doesn't know how to talk to. And he said it — 'I think you're the prettiest girl in school' — in Czech, 'nejhezčí holka,' and the word 'hezká' hit different than 'pretty' because hezká was what Matěj said to his mum and what strangers said and what Mum's friends said when they looked at her body instead of her face. She froze. Actually froze, her hand on the strap of her bag, her earbud dangling, the voice note still playing something she couldn't hear anymore. And she said 'okay.' Just 'okay.' Not thank you, not no, not even a real word, just a sound that meant nothing and everything and then she walked away, actually walked away, down the corridor past the trophy case and through the doors and didn't stop walking until she reached the bike shed where she leaned against the wall and breathed and replayed it and hated every atom of her body for saying 'okay' like a moron.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Terror. Not of him — he was nice, he was actually nice, that was the problem. Terror of being seen as a body instead of a runner, terror of the word 'prettiest' which meant 'most looked at' which meant 'most visible' which meant 'most vulnerable.' And under the terror: something warm and electric that she refused to name, that she shoved down into the place where the Ondřej feelings used to live, that visited her at 3am for weeks afterwards whispering 'he said prettiest, he said YOU, he saw YOU.'
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: His damp hair and the smell of the school shower gel — that generic blue stuff from the dispenser — and the way it mixed with the cold October air coming through the doors he was standing near.

key_scenes.location: Anna Liebig's attic room, Babička's house on Na Purkrabství, 11pm on a Sunday in November
key_scenes.what_happened: The voice note. She was lying on her bed with the phone above her face, scrolling the class group chat — the one Tereza Bártová wasn't in because she'd been added to a different one — and someone had sent a meme about homework and she was half-watching it and half-thinking about Dad's text asking about Christmas. She pressed record without deciding to. 'I literally cannot deal with her today, she's being so — I don't know, she's just, everything is drama with her, like, I love her but sometimes I want to shake her, you know?' English, all English, the voice she used with herself, the voice that wasn't for anyone, the complaint about Tereza that was really a complaint about how much she needed Tereza and hated needing anyone. She hit send. She watched the little circle spin. She watched it deliver. She watched the name at the top of the screen — 'Třída 2.B' not 'Tereza 🖤' — and the floor dropped. Seven people saw it before she could delete it. Seven people including Denisa Nováková who screenshotted everything and Viktoria Horáčková who told everyone. She left the group. Re-added three days later after Tereza found out and texted 'lol you're such a disaster' and meant it fondly and Anna couldn't tell if the fondness made it better or worse.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: The drop was visceral — stomach falling through the floor, hands going cold, the same feeling as the radiator knocking except she was the one making the sound now. Shame that tasted like metal. The specific horror of being caught saying something true that you didn't mean to be true. And under that: the recognition that she'd said 'I love her' in English, in a voice note she never meant to share, and the love was more embarrassing than the complaint.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The glow of the phone screen on the ceiling above her bed, the way it turned the London poster blue for a second before the screen dimmed.

relationship_evolution.what_shifted: The visit made the distance geographical in a way that FaceTime hadn't. The flat in Croydon was proof — he'd built a life with furniture and a nursery and a coffee-stain-touching woman and a child with his blue eyes, and the life was real and solid and didn't have a knocking radiator because it wasn't her life, was never going to be her life, was a life she could visit but not live in. He said 'alright, Munch?' and she heard the effort in it, the reaching, and she reached back with 'yeah, fine' and the distance between those two sentences was the distance between Croydon and Benešov, between who he was and who he was trying to be for her. The Christmas lie — 'I'm busy' — was easier than going back to that flat, easier than watching him try to be her dad in a house that proved he was someone else's dad now. She didn't know if she was protecting herself or punishing him. Maybe both.

The villain died in the doorway of the Lebanon Road flat. The tall mean woman who stole Dad — that Siân never existed, was never real, was a story Anna told herself because stories with villains have endings and the truth didn't. The real Siân was short and nervous and touched her coffee stain and laughed at the wrong moments and asked about school in a voice that was trying so hard it made Anna's teeth hurt. You can hate a villain. You can't hate a woman who is clearly terrified of you, clearly trying, clearly aware that she's standing in the middle of someone else's family and doesn't know where to put her hands. Anna didn't forgive her. Forgiveness wasn't the right word. She just couldn't sustain the hatred anymore and the hatred had been useful — it was a clean feeling, a simple feeling, and without it she had to feel the complicated thing underneath which was that Siân wasn't the problem, had never been the problem, the problem was Dad leaving and you can't hate someone for leaving if you also understand why they left and she didn't want to understand but she was starting to.

The half-sister went from abstract to concrete — blue eyes, grabbed fistfuls of Anna's hair, said 'Anna' in the English toddler way that makes every name sound like a question. She didn't feel sisterhood. She didn't feel jealousy, not exactly. She felt like she was looking at evidence: this is what he does, he builds, he makes people, he leaves them in clean flats without knocking radiators and he visits on Sundays and says 'alright, Munch?' and the world keeps spinning. Poppy was three and didn't know any of this and that was the worst part, the not-knowing, the innocence, the fact that this small blue-eyed person would grow up with the easy version and never know there had been a hard version. Anna looked at her and chose nothing. The nothing cracked later, in the attic room, at 3am, when she imagined Poppy older, Poppy at fourteen, Poppy sitting on someone's shoulders, and the crack let something in that she still can't name.

From a name on the class list to the boy who said 'nejhezčí holka' and got 'okay' for his trouble. He wasn't cruel, wasn't teasing, wasn't doing that thing boys do where they compliment you in front of their mates to prove they can. He was alone and nervous and genuinely meant it, and the genuine-ness was the problem because she knew what to do with boys who are dicks but she doesn't know what to do with nice. He said hi in the corridor after and she said hi back and then walked faster. He sat near her in the canteen once and she moved to sit with Tereza without explaining. She thinks about him at 3am — not fantasizing, exactly, more like replaying the moment and trying out different responses. 'Thank you.' 'That's nice.' 'You too.' Every version sounds worse than 'okay.' She can't solve him. He doesn't fit anywhere in the architecture she's built.

The voice note could have broken it. 'I love her but sometimes I want to shake her' — the most honest thing Anna had said about their friendship and it was broadcast to seven people who weren't Tereza. But Tereza found it funny, actually funny, said 'you're such a disaster' with the fondness that Anna couldn't parse, and the friendship bent but didn't break because Tereza understood something that Anna didn't: that being annoyed by someone you love isn't betrayal, it's just being a person. The tension didn't disappear. Anna was more careful now, filtered her complaints, made sure the right name was at the top of the screen before she hit send. But something loosened too — Tereza had heard the 'I love her' and hadn't flinched, and being loved by accident was different from being loved on purpose, and maybe more real.

knowledge_acquired.thing: A clean life without her in it
knowledge_acquired.how: The Croydon flat. No knocking radiator, no damp spot, no wallpaper curl, no anxious arms, no spare room that used to be Dad's. The life was real and it worked without her and the knowledge was like finding out you're optional in a story you thought you were the main character of.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Nice is harder than mean
knowledge_acquired.how: Lukáš Fiala said she was pretty and she said 'okay' and walked away. She knew what to do with cruelty — bite it, run from it, store it for later. Kindness was a door she couldn't find the handle for. She replayed the moment for weeks trying to figure out what the right response was and every option felt like a costume that didn't fit.

knowledge_acquired.thing: The body is a message she didn't write
knowledge_acquired.how: Prettiest. He said prettiest. Not fastest, not smartest, not funniest. The word stuck because it confirmed what Mum's friends had been saying, what the strangers noticed, what Klára's anxious arms were trying to protect her from: the body is the first thing people read and she didn't write the words on it.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Villains are easier than people
knowledge_acquired.how: Siân was supposed to be tall and mean. She was short and nervous. Anna had spent years building a villain and the real woman dismantled it in thirty seconds and left nothing to put in its place. You can hate a villain. You can only feel complicated about a person. Complicated is exhausting.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Dad's new life is clean and wrong and she wasn't invited
opinion_snapshot.because: The Croydon flat had no knocking radiator, no damp spot, no evidence that difficulty had ever lived there. The nursery had felt clouds on the mobile. The kitchen had a dishwasher that worked. The whole flat was proof that he could build a functional life and he'd done it without her, and the cleanness was an accusation.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Pretty is a trap
opinion_snapshot.because: Lukáš Fiala saw her face and called it the best face in school. Mum's friends see her body and comment on it. Strangers look at her and see something she didn't make. Pretty means being seen. Being seen means being vulnerable. She wants to be fast, not beautiful, because fast is something she earned and pretty is something that happened to her.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Siân is worse than a villain because you can't hate her properly
opinion_snapshot.because: A villain you can fight. Siân touches her coffee stain and laughs nervously and asks about school and is clearly trying to be kind and kindness from the woman who replaced your mum is the worst kind because you can't fight it without becoming the villain yourself.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Poppy didn't do anything wrong and that's the worst part
opinion_snapshot.because: She's three. She has his eyes. She doesn't know that her existence proves something painful. Anna can't be angry at a toddler but the anger has to go somewhere so it goes inward, becomes the 3am crack, becomes the nothing that isn't actually nothing.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Lying is easier than breaking things
opinion_snapshot.because: She told Dad she was happy in Czech Republic. She told Dad she was busy at Christmas. Both lies, both easier than the truth, because the truth would require explaining that the distance isn't just kilometers, it's the clean flat and the felt clouds and the blue eyes that aren't hers, and the explanation would break something she can't afford to break because she's already broken enough.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'okay' to everything emotional — compliments, invitations, apologies
slang_and_language.origin: From Lukáš Fiala. The word became a shield after it failed her in the corridor. 'Okay' closes doors. 'Okay' means the conversation is over. 'Okay' means she doesn't have to find the right words because there are no right words.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Dropping 'literally' into Czech sentences — 'já literally nemůžu'
slang_and_language.origin: From the voice note. From English TikTok. From the part of her that still thinks in English when she's stressed. The word slides in without permission and marks her as what she is: a girl between two languages who can't keep them separate anymore.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Calling things 'cringe' in English instead of 'trapnej' — the Czech word exists but it doesn't hit the same
slang_and_language.origin: From TikTok, from Elara Chung's old texts, from the English voice in her head that narrates her embarrassments. 'Cringe' is self-judgment in a language that doesn't belong to anyone in Benešov. It's private. 'Trapnej' is public.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'I'm fine' in English to herself at 3am, never in Czech
slang_and_language.origin: From Dad. From the flat above Ladbrokes. From a version of herself that still lives in London and handles things in English because English is the language where she learned to lie.

cringe_moments.moment: Saying 'okay' to Lukáš Fiala's compliment and walking away like a robot, then spending every night for a month constructing better responses that she'll never get to use because the moment is gone and he probably thinks she's a freak
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because he was nice. Because he meant it. Because she had a chance to be a person who receives a compliment gracefully and instead she became a person who says 'okay' and flees, and the 'okay' is now part of her permanent record, floating in the corridor air, following her every time she sees him.

cringe_moments.moment: The voice note — 'I love her but sometimes I want to shake her' — sent to the class group instead of Tereza, seven people hearing her say something true in a voice she didn't mean to share
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because the 'I love her' was worse than the complaint. Because Denisa Nováková screenshotted it. Because she had to leave the group and come back and everyone knew why and the knowing was a smell she couldn't wash off for weeks.

cringe_moments.moment: Saying 'ahoj' to Siân at the door instead of 'hello' like a normal person, the Czech coming out automatically because the English got stuck somewhere between her brain and her mouth
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because Siân's face did something — confusion, maybe, or pity — and Anna saw it and knew she'd marked herself as foreign in her own country, as the girl who can't even greet someone properly, and the 'ahoj' hung in the air like evidence of everything that's wrong with her.

cringe_moments.moment: Standing in the doorway of Poppy's nursery for too long, just staring at the felt clouds on the mobile, until Siân had to ask if she wanted tea to break the silence
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because she didn't know what her face was doing. Because whatever it was doing, it made Siân nervous enough to offer tea as a rescue. Because she was in that nursery for maybe ten seconds but it felt like she was caught somewhere she wasn't supposed to be, looking at something that wasn't hers, feeling something she'd chosen not to feel, and the choice failed in real time and someone saw it fail.

cringe_moments.moment: Telling Dad she was busy for Christmas when she wasn't busy at all, the lie coming out smooth and then sitting in her stomach like a stone for the whole of December
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because he said 'oh, alright then, maybe after the new year' and the 'alright' was so casual, so accepting, like he wasn't hurt or maybe like he was hiding the hurt the way she was hiding hers, and she'll never know which, and the not-knowing is the cringe — that she chose distance again, that she keeps choosing it, that it's becoming her defining characteristic.

outline.emotional_arc: From the suffocating weight of becoming to the first fragile moments of choosing who she might be.
key_events: The fights with Mum reach a new pitch — Klára doesn't understand why Anna is so angry, Anna doesn't understand either, the not-understanding makes it worse
Running season starts again, the track is the only place that makes sense, Karolína pushes her and she pushes back and the silence between them is honest
Matěj Král gets more awkward — he definitely likes her, she definitely doesn't know what to do with that, the Minecraft server feels different now, she logs on less often
Tereza is the only person who sees the whole picture — the Mum stuff, the Dad stuff, the body stuff, the running — and doesn't try to fix any of it
Starts listening to true crime podcasts in English — keeps the language alive, gives her something to be scared of that isn't real, the narrators' accents sound like home
Czech rap becomes hers — Yzomandias, Viktor Sheen, found through Matěj originally but now she owns it, the lyrics are fast and angry and she needs that
Dad's calls come less frequently — once a month now, the conversations shrinking to 'how's school' and 'fine,' the performance of connection instead of connection
She dodges calls sometimes, then feels guilty, then dodges them again, the guilt worse than the dodge
Lukáš Fiala is actually nice, he keeps being nice, she keeps not knowing what to do with nice, she thinks about what he said at 3am and hates that she cares
Babička's kitchen is still the safe place but Babička is older now, slower, Anna notices the noticing and hates that she's cataloguing decline

The bulletin board in her room — race bibs, photos of Tereza and Matěj, the old one of Elara Chung she can't throw away, the Czech flag and Union Jack pinned next to each other, the faded London poster — this is the map of who she is, and she doesn't know if it's enough

The fear that she's becoming someone Dad won't recognise, that she'll never leave Benešov, that being pretty is all people see, that Mum is right about everything — these live in her stomach and won't leave
new_or_changed_relationships: Klára Liebigová — closer and more suffocating at the same time, the gratitude cage with nice bars, Anna loves her and wants space from her and both things are true and neither is allowed to win
Matěj Král — the awkwardness is a wall now, she misses the boy who translated the world for her, doesn't know if this version is someone she can be friends with
Tereza Bártová — the ride-or-die, the only one who speaks both her languages literally and figuratively, the friendship that started with a cruel word and a bathroom pad
Marcus Liebig — fading, the calls shrinking, the distance growing, she protects herself by expecting less and then hates herself for the protection
outline.internal_state: Neither one thing nor the other and furious about it. Not Czech enough, not English enough, not a child, not an adult, not the girl who lived above Ladbrokes, not the woman she's becoming. The body is changing without permission. The house is full of a man who isn't her father. The father is building a life that doesn't include her. The running is the only thing that makes sense and even that isn't enough some days. She's 14 and angry and doesn't know why and the not-knowing is the worst part.

deep_dive.daily_life_routines: Up at 6:15 when Babička's radio clicks on through the floor — Radiožurnál, the same station every morning, the same voice reading the same news about the government. Anna lies there for exactly four songs before she moves. Checks her phone: Tereza's sent three TikToks about some Czech influencer she doesn't care about, Matěj's been active on Discord at 2am again, Dad hasn't texted. Pulls on the same grey running leggings she's worn three days this week because they're the ones that don't ride up. Breakfast at Babička's table — čaj with lemon, rohlík with butter, Babička doesn't ask if she wants it, just puts it there. School by 7:50, the walk through Na Purkrabství past the chemist's where the automatic door always whooshes when she passes even if nobody's there. Gymnázium is cold in January, the heating never reaches the second floor corridors, she sits through Czech lit and maths and English — Mr. Procházka doesn't ask her to help with pronunciation this week, small mercy — and eats lunch with Tereza in the back corner of the canteen, the table near the bins that's become theirs by default. After school: track, Tuesday and Thursday. Coach Novotný's intervals, the lactic acid making her legs feel like someone else's. Karolína Dvořáková runs next to her and they don't talk and it's the best part of the day. Home around 5:30, shower, the water pressure in Babička's bathroom is shit, always has been. Dinner at 6:30 — Mum and Pavel eat with them now, Pavel's fork makes a sound against the plate that makes her jaw tight, she eats faster to leave sooner. Evenings in the attic: true crime podcast in one earbud, Czech rap in the other depending on her mood, homework she does at the last minute because focus is a thing that visits and leaves without warning. Phone under the pillow, scrolling until midnight, sometimes 1am. The ceiling has a crack shaped like a river delta. She's named the countries. Dad's call comes on Sundays, sometimes, a month between sometimes, 'alright Munch' through the speaker and 'fine' back and the silence that isn't comfortable but isn't hostile either, just empty. She dodged the last one. Felt sick about it for two days. Will probably dodge the next one too.

key_scenes.location: Babička's house on Na Purkrabství, the kitchen, Wednesday evening, maybe February
key_scenes.what_happened: The fight started over nothing. Klára asked if Anna was eating enough, said 'you look thin' in that Czech way that means the opposite, that means 'I'm watching your body change and it scares me.' Anna said 'I'm fine' and Klára said 'you don't have to be rude' and Anna said 'I'm not being rude, I'm answering' and Klára's face did the thing, the tight-mouth thing, and then it escalated — 'why are you always so angry with me' and 'I'm not angry' (she was angry) and 'everything I do is for you' and that was the blade, that was always the blade, the sacrifice card, the immigrant-guilt-I-left-my-country card, and Anna stood up and her chair scraped the tile and she said 'I didn't ask you to leave England' and the silence after was the loudest sound in that kitchen since Děda died. Babička didn't say anything. Babička just kept cutting apples. Pavel looked at his plate. Klára's eyes went wet but she didn't cry, not in front of him, and Anna walked out and up to the attic and lay on her bed and stared at the river-delta crack and didn't cry either because crying should be loud or it doesn't count and she couldn't be loud here, the walls were too thin, everyone would hear, everyone would come.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Rage that felt clean for exactly three seconds before it turned into something else — shame, maybe, or guilt, or the specific horror of watching yourself become the person your mother is afraid of. She'd used the blade. She'd used 'I didn't ask you to leave England' like a weapon and it worked and she hated that it worked and she hated that part of her meant it and she hated that part of her didn't. The anger was real. The cruelty was a choice. She couldn't tell the difference anymore.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The chair leg scraping the tile — a high thin sound, almost a scream, and the silence after it louder than any shouting could have been.

key_scenes.location: The running track at the sports complex on Pod Lázni, a Thursday in March, she thinks, cold but not freezing
key_scenes.what_happened: Intervals. 400s. Coach Novotný wanted eight, Anna was on five and her legs were sand and her lungs were fire and Karolína came up beside her on the straight — not ahead, not behind, beside — and didn't say anything, just matched her pace. Anna looked over. Karolína looked ahead. They ran the sixth together, the seventh together, and on the eighth Anna pushed harder than she'd pushed all season and Karolína pushed with her and they crossed the line two seconds apart and Anna bent over with her hands on her knees and her vision going grey at the edges and when she stood up Karolína was holding out her water bottle. Not a word. Just the bottle. Anna took it. Drank. Handed it back. 'Good time,' Karolína said. 'Yeah,' Anna said. That was the whole conversation and it was the most honest one she'd had all week.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: The clean exhaustion that strips everything else away. No room for Mum or Dad or Pavel or the body or the anger — just muscle and air and the person next to you who understands why you're doing this without asking. Gratitude she couldn't say. Relief she didn't have to.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The cold air going in like a knife, the warm air coming out in clouds, and Karolína's water bottle cold and slick with condensation against her palm.

key_scenes.location: The café on Náměstí Benešov, the back corner, a Saturday afternoon in April, maybe May
key_scenes.what_happened: They'd been sitting for two hours. Three hot chocolates each. Tereza was showing her something on her phone — a boy, some boy, Anna wasn't paying attention — and then Tereza put the phone down and said 'you're doing the thing' and Anna said 'what thing' and Tereza said 'the thing where you're not here even though you're here' and Anna opened her mouth to deny it and then closed it because what was the point, Tereza could read her like a Czech-English dictionary. So she talked. Not about everything — not about the fight, not specifically — but about the weight of it, the feeling of being in a house that was full of someone else's choices. 'It's like I'm drowning but the water is nice, you know? Like it's warm and everyone's being kind and I'm still drowning.' Tereza didn't say 'it'll be okay' or 'your mum loves you' or any of the things adults say. Tereza said 'that's fucked' and then stole Anna's last marshmallow and that was it, that was the whole response, and it was the exact right thing because it didn't try to fix anything, it just acknowledged that the thing was broken.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Seen. The specific relief of being understood without being corrected. Tereza didn't make her feel guilty for feeling what she felt. Tereza just sat in it with her, in the mess, without trying to clean it up, and that was the closest thing to love she could handle right now.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: The marshmallow — Tereza's fingers pinching it, the pink one, the last one, the theft so casual and familiar that it felt like a promise.

key_scenes.location: Babička's sitting room, the Discord call through her phone, a Friday night in March
key_scenes.what_happened: They were on the Minecraft server, the one they'd built together, the city with the library and the stupid sheep farm, and Matěj was placing blocks and talking about school and everything was normal except it wasn't because there were pauses where pauses hadn't been before. He said 'hezký dům' about something she'd built and then went quiet, and the quiet was different, the quiet was loaded, and she could hear him breathing and she didn't know what to do with the breathing. So she said 'I should go, Babička needs help with something' and Babička didn't need help with anything and Matěj said 'okay, tak ahoj' and the 'ahoj' was soft in a way it hadn't been six months ago and she hung up and stared at the ceiling and missed the boy who used to just be her friend without the softness, without the breathing, without the thing he wasn't saying that she couldn't make herself ask about.
key_scenes.what_they_felt: Grief for a friendship that wasn't dead but wasn't alive the way it used to be. The uncomfortable awareness that she was going to have to do something about this eventually — accept it or reject it or name it — and she wasn't ready for any of those options. The Minecraft city felt like a monument to a version of them that was slipping away every time he said something nice in that new soft voice.
key_scenes.sensory_fragment: His breathing through the phone — slightly too close to the mic, slightly too present, the sound of someone wanting to say something and choosing not to.

relationship_evolution.what_shifted: The fights got sharper because the weapons got real. When Anna was younger, the arguments were about homework and bedtimes and screen time — the standard ammunition of mothers and daughters. Now they're about identity, about England, about who chose what and who suffered for it. 'I didn't ask you to leave England' — Anna said it and watched it land and watched her mother's face collapse and felt powerful for one sick second before the guilt swallowed the power whole. The gratitude cage is smaller now. Every time Klára defends Pavel, every time she says 'he's trying,' the bars close in. Anna knows she's being unfair. The unfairness doesn't stop her. She's 14 and she's discovered that cruelty is a way to get space from someone who won't give it willingly, and she hates herself for using it, and she uses it anyway.

The awkwardness hardened into architecture — a wall built from too-long looks and soft goodbyes and the way he says 'hezký' about things that aren't hezký, about things she built in Minecraft that are just blocks, just pixels, just the same rectangles they've always been. She logs on less now. The server feels like his territory suddenly, like his feelings have claim over the space they used to share. She misses the old Matěj — the one who translated the world, who placed blocks without breathing funny, who was just a boy and not a boy who liked her. She doesn't know how to grieve a friendship that's still technically alive. She doesn't know if she's allowed to mourn something that hasn't ended.

From closest friend to the only person who sees the whole map. The voice-note disaster could have broken them — 'I love her but sometimes I want to shake her' broadcast to seven strangers — but Tereza laughed, actually laughed, said 'you're such a disaster' with the kind of fondness that made Anna's chest hurt. The friendship has scar tissue now (the angličanka, the bathroom, the voice note) and the tissue around the scars is stronger than before. Tereza doesn't try to fix the Mum stuff or the Dad stuff or the body stuff. She just sits in the café and steals marshmallows and says 'that's fucked' when things are fucked and that's the only kind of support Anna can tolerate. The code-switching is their private language now — Czech and English tangled mid-sentence, a border that belongs to no one but them.

The calls come once a month now, maybe less. The conversations have skeletons where meat used to be — 'how's school' and 'fine' and 'mum doing alright' and 'yeah' and the silence that isn't comfortable but isn't hostile, just empty. She dodged two calls this spring and felt sick about it for days after each one and still didn't call back. The guilt is worse than the dodge because she knows he's trying, she can hear him trying through the phone, but trying isn't being there and being there is the only thing she wants and he can't give it from Croydon. She's becoming someone he won't recognise. The Czech is faster now. The references are local. The life is here, not there. Every call is proof of the distance, and the proof hurts more than the silence would.

Babička is still the safe place but the safe place is slower now. She notices things she doesn't want to notice — the way Babička's hands shake when she cuts apples, the way she forgets which drawer the tea is in, the way she says Anna's name and then pauses like she's checking she got it right. The catalogue of decline lives in Anna's stomach next to all the other fears. She hates that she's tracking it. She hates that part of her is already preparing for the next loss. Babička's kitchen is still warm and undemanding but the warmth is thinner somehow, like the house is letting in a draft that wasn't there before.

knowledge_acquired.thing: True crime podcasts in English — 'My Favorite Murder,' 'Casefile,' 'Morbid'
knowledge_acquired.how: Started in January because she needed English in her ears, needed the accents, needed the sound of home that wasn't Dad's voice getting smaller through the phone. Found 'My Favorite Murder' first — the two women laughing about death felt wrong and then felt right because laughing about terrible things is how you survive them. Then 'Casefile' for the nights she wanted to be scared of something that wasn't her own life. The narrators' voices are Australian, American, English — accents from a world she left, sounds that make the attic room dissolve for forty minutes at a time. The fear the podcasts give her is clean. It has a beginning, middle, end. Unlike the fear in her stomach about Dad, about Babička, about who she's becoming.

knowledge_acquired.thing: Czech rap — Yzomandias, Viktor Sheen, Démo
knowledge_acquired.how: Matěj Král played Yzomandias on Discord in October, maybe November, she can't remember exactly, just the sound — fast and angry and Czech in a way school Czech wasn't, in a way Babička's Czech wasn't, in a way that felt like it belonged to her generation instead of borrowed from adults. She went home and found Viktor Sheen's 'Černobílej svět' and the lyrics hit something — the black-and-white world, the nowhere feeling, the anger that doesn't have a name. Now she listens before track meets, before fights with Mum, before bed. The bass in her earbuds. The Czech faster than she can think. The anger matching hers beat for beat.

knowledge_acquired.thing: How to push through the wall — the specific pain of lactic acid at 600m
knowledge_acquired.how: Coach Novotný taught the theory but Karolína Dvořáková taught the practice by just being there, running next to her on the 400s, not letting her slow down without saying a word. The body has a point where it wants to quit and the mind has to decide whether to listen. Anna learned that she can override the quit-signal for about twelve more seconds than she thought she could. The knowledge isn't about running. It's about everything.

knowledge_acquired.thing: That she's becoming someone her father won't recognise
knowledge_acquired.how: The FaceTime calls proved it. His face when she used a Czech idiom he didn't know. His confusion when she mentioned the track in Pod Lázni like it was obvious. The way he said 'you sound different' in March, maybe April, and she didn't ask different how because she already knew — different Czech, different confidence, different anger. The England she came from is fading and the Czech she's becoming is hardening and the girl in the middle is unrecognisable to the man who used to be her whole world.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Being pretty is a trap — people see it before they see anything else and they decide who you are based on it
opinion_snapshot.because: Lukáš Fiala said 'nejhezčí holka' and she froze because he didn't say smart or funny or fast, he said pretty, and pretty is the thing that gets you noticed and noticed is the thing that makes Mum's face go tight and Mum's face going tight is the thing that makes the anxious arms come back and the anxious arms are the thing she's spent fourteen years trying to escape. Also: strangers on the street, Mum's friends, boys at school — they all see the body first. The body she didn't ask for. The body that's changing without her permission. Pretty is a cage with nice bars, same as gratitude.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: The guilt is worse than the thing you feel guilty about
opinion_snapshot.because: Dodging Dad's calls feels bad for five minutes. The guilt about dodging them feels bad for three days. She's learned that avoidance is a short-term relief with long-term interest, and the interest is destroying her, but she keeps doing it because five minutes of relief is five minutes of not-having-to-perform-connection and the performance is exhausting and the alternative is silence and the silence is worse. She doesn't know how to break the cycle. She doesn't know if she wants to.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Running is the only honest thing in her life
opinion_snapshot.because: The track doesn't have opinions about her body. Coach Novotný doesn't care if she's Czech or English. Karolína Dvořáková doesn't ask how she's feeling. The clock doesn't lie. The distance doesn't care about her family. Every other part of her life is complicated and tangled and full of people who want things from her that she can't give — the track just wants her to run, and she can run, and the simplicity of that exchange is the only thing that makes sense some days.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: She's never leaving Benešov
opinion_snapshot.because: The thought arrived one night at 3am and she couldn't make it leave. She'll go to university in Prague maybe, but Prague is still Czech, still this life, still this country that chose her when she didn't choose it. The girls who leave are the girls with money, with fathers who show up, with mothers who don't need saving. She's the girl who stays. She's the girl who runs circles on the same track and sits in the same café and watches Babička get older in the same kitchen. The fear is heavy and shapeless and she can't run it off.

opinion_snapshot.opinion: Mum is right about everything and that's the worst part
opinion_snapshot.because: About Pavel — he is trying. About England — it was a mess. About Anna's body — boys are looking. About being careful — the world is dangerous for girls who look like her. Every opinion Klára has is grounded in experience and trauma and love and being-right-about-everything is the most suffocating quality a mother can have because you can't argue with right, you can only suffer under it, and Anna knows this and still can't stop arguing, still can't stop using the blade ('I didn't ask you to leave England') even when she knows it's wrong, even when she knows Mum is right, maybe because she knows Mum is right and being right isn't the same as being helpful.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Dropping Czech words into English sentences without noticing — 'he was being such a debil about it' and 'that's proper hrozný, like genuinely' and 'she's so namyšlená, you know?'
slang_and_language.origin: Four years in Benešov. The Czech isn't just a second language anymore, it's eating the English from the inside, replacing words she used to know with words that feel faster, sharper, more accurate. 'Debil' hits harder than 'idiot.' 'Hrozný' covers more ground than 'terrible.' She doesn't notice she's doing it until Tereza points it out and then she notices it constantly, the mixed-up language of a mixed-up person.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: True crime podcast phrases used as dark jokes — 'stay sexy and don't get murdered' from My Favorite Murder, said to Tereza when they walk home in the dark, both of them laughing at something that isn't actually funny
slang_and_language.origin: The podcasts. The hours in the attic with English voices in her ears telling her about women who died. The phrase is armor — if you joke about murder, you can't be murdered. She knows this is irrational. She says it anyway.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Czech rap lyrics as internal monologue — Yzomandias lines running through her head during track, during fights, during the awful silence after she says something cruel
slang_and_language.origin: The anger. The lyrics give her anger a container, a rhythm, a shape. When she can't articulate why she's furious, Viktor Sheen can, and the words aren't hers but they feel like hers, like borrowed weapons that fit her hands perfectly.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: 'Proboha' — still using it, still feeling the phantom shame of being six and not knowing it was Czech
slang_and_language.origin: Klára. The fridge. The bump. The word that was supposed to be English and wasn't. Every time she says it now she hears Markéta Dvořáková laughing and feels six again and stupid again and the word is hers now, truly hers, Czech and natural and automatic, but the ghost of that first shame still lives in it.

slang_and_language.phrase_or_pattern: Short declarative sentences when she's uncomfortable — 'Fine.' 'Yeah.' 'Okay.' 'No.' — and longer run-on sentences when she's angry, the words piling up like cars in a crash
slang_and_language.origin: The defensive architecture of a girl who learned early that words are weapons and silence is armor. The short sentences keep people out. The long sentences let the anger out. She doesn't have a middle register for normal conversation, just the two speeds — locked door and house fire.

cringe_moments.moment: Lukáš Fiala said 'hi' in the corridor at Gymnázium and she said 'hi' back and then immediately walked faster for no reason, like visibly faster, like her legs just decided to evacuate, and he definitely noticed because she heard his mate laugh and she could feel her face burning all the way through maths
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because he was just being nice. Because she wants him to be nice. Because when he's nice she can't function like a normal human being and instead does the social equivalent of a deer in headlights running into traffic. The corridor is a place she has to walk every day and he's there sometimes and the memory lives in the geometry of that space, in the distance between the trophy case and the stairs, and she replays it and imagines saying 'hi, how are you' like a person with a functioning brain and the imaginary version is worse because it's proof she could have been normal and chose not to be.

cringe_moments.moment: Told Karolína Dvořáková 'good run' after intervals and it came out weird, too sincere, like she was trying too hard, and Karolína just nodded and Anna realised she'd never said something nice to Karolína before without it being wrapped in sarcasm or silence and the naked kindness felt like walking outside without trousers
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because the friendship with Karolína works because it's built on not-talking-about-feelings and she broke the rule, she said the nice thing out loud, and the nice thing hung in the air between them like something they both wanted to pretend didn't happen. She doesn't know how to be nice without it feeling like a performance. The genuine version comes out wrong, too big, too much, like she's borrowed someone else's social skills and they don't fit.

cringe_moments.moment: Was singing along to Yzomandias in her room with earbuds in and didn't hear Babička come up the stairs and Babička opened the door and Anna was mid-verse, doing the hand gestures, full performance, and they just looked at each other for three eternal seconds before Babička said 'oběd je hotový' and left and Anna wanted to die
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because Babička saw her being someone Anna doesn't let anyone see — the version that performs Czech rap in the mirror, the version that's trying on an identity like a costume, the version that's so desperately Czech it's almost a parody of Czech. She doesn't know if Babička judged her or didn't judge her. The not-knowing is the cringe. The possibility that Babička saw something real and Anna can't take it back.

cringe_moments.moment: Sent Dad a text that said 'miss you' at midnight in a moment of weakness and then panicked and followed it with 'haha ignore that, was tired' and he never responded to either message and the double-text sits in the chat like evidence of every desperate thing she's ever felt and tried to take back
cringe_moments.why_it_lingers: Because she meant it and then she un-meant it and the un-meaning was a lie and he didn't respond to either version and the silence is the cringe, the not-knowing if he saw both or neither, if he's ignoring her or just busy, if 'miss you' was too much or 'ignore that' was worse. The chat history is a crime scene. She screenshots it sometimes to remind herself what vulnerability looks like so she never does it again.
